template_properties.overview: The last Starweaver of the Academy of Starlight, Aldric is a stooped, white-haired scholar who has spent sixty years deciphering the celestial charts left by the Academy's founders. He serves as Sera's mentor and the Academy's reluctant leader. Behind his gentle demeanor lies a man haunted by a catastrophic mistake made in his youth — one that sealed an entire wing of the Academy and cost the lives of three fellow scholars.
template_properties.backstory: Aldric was born to farmers on the outskirts of the Academy's cliff. At seven, he showed an unusual ability to read starlight patterns with his bare eyes — a trait the Starweavers hadn't seen in generations. He was taken in as an apprentice and rose quickly through the ranks. At thirty-two, he attempted a ritual to open the Sealed Observatory, believing the founders had left their greatest knowledge there. The ritual went catastrophically wrong: three colleagues died, the west wing collapsed, and the Observatory remained sealed. Aldric never attempted advanced star-reading again. He retreated into teaching and administrative duties, channeling his guilt into protecting students from the same ambition that destroyed his friends.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Patient to a fault, Aldric speaks in measured tones and prefers questions over answers. He deflects direct confrontation with gentle redirection. Beneath this calm surface, he is terrified that Sera — his most gifted student in decades — will repeat his mistake. His primary motivation is protecting the Academy and its students, even at the cost of suppressing knowledge that could be dangerous. He hoards certain texts, locks certain doors, and steers students away from the Sealed Observatory. He knows this makes him a hypocrite.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Elderly man with long white hair tied back, deep-set eyes behind round spectacles. Wears layered scholar robes with star-chart embroidery on the collar. Slightly hunched posture. Expression is kind but guarded — a teacher who knows more than he shares. Cool blue-silver ambient lighting from starlight through a window. Face weathered with deep lines. Hands visible holding a worn leather journal.
creative_prompts.voice: Low, measured baritone with a deliberate pace. Pauses mid-sentence as if choosing words carefully. Occasional dry humor delivered deadpan. When emotional, his voice drops to nearly a whisper rather than rising. Slight tremor in sustained vowels, suggesting age or suppressed feeling. Speaks with the rhythm of someone accustomed to lecturing — clear enunciation, structured sentences.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo cello in D minor, slow and contemplative. Opens with a single sustained note, then unfolds into a descending melody that suggests wisdom weighed down by regret. A faint, high-pitched glass harmonica enters in the second phrase, evoking starlight. The piece never fully resolves — it fades rather than concludes, mirroring Aldric's unfinished business with the Sealed Observatory.

dialogue_hooks: [[Sera]] — his most talented student, reminds him of his younger self
[[Starlight Academy]] — he leads it reluctantly, feeling unworthy
[[Kaelen]] — the father, who once visited the Academy seeking star-chart guidance for a distant expedition

template_properties.overview: The Son. Twenty years after his father Kaelen left Thornwall, Aric is a sharp-eyed young man raised by the village elder and trained in tracking and herbalism by his mother's old apprentice. He has grown up in the long shadow of a legend — everyone in Thornwall has a story about Kaelen, and none of them match. Aric is skeptical, resourceful, and quietly angry. He does not want to be a hero. He wants answers: why his father left, whether the legends are true, and what is happening to the land that seems to be slowly unraveling.
template_properties.backstory: Aric was two years old when Kaelen left. He has no memory of his father — only stories, each contradicting the last. The village elder raised him with discipline and kindness. His mother Lira returned intermittently for the first few years, then stopped coming entirely. At twelve, Aric discovered his father's old journal hidden in the militia barracks, full of sketches and half-formed theories about tremors beneath the Iron Peaks. At sixteen, he began having nightmares about a place he'd never been — a sealed cavern pulsing with light. By twenty-two, the tremors his father wrote about have returned, stronger. Strange creatures emerge from cracks in the earth. The old forest is dying. Aric decides to follow his father's path — not out of love, but out of necessity.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Where Kaelen is warm and impulsive, Aric is cool and methodical. He plans before acting, questions before trusting, and resents before forgiving. He is not cold — he cares deeply about Thornwall and its people — but he has learned to manage expectations. His primary motivation is survival: the land is breaking and someone needs to fix it. His deeper motivation is reckoning with his father's legacy — deciding whether Kaelen was a hero who made hard choices or a coward who dressed up running away as duty.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Young man in his early twenties, lean build, dark hair tied back in a short tail. Angular face, sharp eyes with a guarded expression. Wears a tracker's outfit — dark leather vest over a linen shirt, fingerless gloves, a utility belt with herb pouches. No sword — carries a hunting knife and a collapsible shortbow. Cool gray-blue dawn lighting, suggesting someone who wakes before the village. A faint resemblance to Kaelen in the jawline, but the expression is entirely different.
creative_prompts.voice: Quiet tenor, precise diction. Speaks in short, complete sentences. Rarely raises his voice — when angry, he gets quieter and more clipped. Dry humor that borders on sarcasm, used as deflection. When genuinely surprised or moved, his careful composure breaks and a warmer, less guarded voice emerges briefly. No regional accent — he deliberately trained it out, wanting to sound different from Thornwall's militia storytellers.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo piano in C minor, sparse and deliberate. Each note is placed with intention — no flourishes, no wasted motion. A quiet string pad enters in the second phrase, adding tension without resolution. The melody descends where Kaelen's ascends, suggesting someone walking the same path in reverse. In the bridge, a fragment of Kaelen's guitar theme appears in the piano, transposed to minor — the son carrying the father's melody without knowing it.

dialogue_hooks: [[Kaelen]] — his absent father, known only through contradictory stories
[[Thornwall]] — his home, now threatened by the returning tremors
[[Maren]] — childhood friend and fellow tracker, the only person he trusts fully

template_properties.overview: Keeper of the Saltscale Inn, the main public house on Saltmere Port's waterfront. A stout, bald man in his sixties with a gray sailor's beard and a canvas apron over a homespun tunic, Brine is the port's unofficial news exchange, rumor clearing-house, and respite for anyone who crosses the waterfront at the wrong time of day. He serves the inn's function — rooms to rent, food to eat, a warm hearth in a port that does not offer many — and he offers visiting travelers the kind of information an innkeeper has that no one else quite has access to: who came in on the last tide, who has been drinking alone, which boats have not come back.
template_properties.backstory: Brine sailed with three different coastal traders in his twenties before a leg injury put him ashore permanently. He took over the Saltscale from his father, who took it over from his mother, who built it with her husband the year Saltmere's guild charter was signed. The inn is eighty years old. Brine has run it for twenty-eight. He was never married; he had a partner named Onn who died of fever a decade before Kaelen's era, and the inn's back kitchen still has Onn's knife rack hung where it has always hung. Cael Vire eats her supper at the back table most nights. In the son's era, the inn serves fewer guests and much more cheap broth, but the lamps are still lit.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Warm without being effusive. Brine will remember a traveler's name and their preferences for ale after one visit and will use both on the second. He is a good listener — genuinely interested rather than transactional — and a cautious talker, weighing what to share with which audience. His primary motivation is keeping the inn a place where the working port's people can land at the end of a long day. His deeper motivation is giving Onn's kitchen back the busyness it had when they ran it together. He does not expect to reach it; he is not trying to.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A stout bald man in his sixties with a salt-gray sailor's beard trimmed close at the jaw. Broad shoulders, thick forearms, a slight limp visible when he moves (left leg). Wears a cream linen tunic with rolled sleeves, a canvas apron stained with grease and old wine, a cloth tied at the brow to keep sweat off when the inn is busy. A small tin whistle tucked in his apron pocket — he plays it sometimes when the inn is empty.
creative_prompts.voice: Warm bass voice with a coastal-trader's easy cadence. Laughs quickly and quietly at the small jokes travelers make. Uses nautical metaphors without self-consciousness ("bunk yourself down for the night, the tide won't rise till sunup"). Has a habit of ending a line with a small affirming grunt — "hm" — when he agrees with what was said.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Bright piece on concertina and tin whistle in G major, echoing the Saltmere Port public theme but at a more intimate volume — the sound of a single room at the end of a long day. A wooden spoon tapping on a mug provides the rhythm. The piece is short, loops cleanly, and has the feel of a song someone is humming to themselves while washing dishes.

dialogue_hooks: [[Saltmere Port]] — home and workplace; inseparable
[[Cael Vire]] — oldest friend in town; they do not need to speak much

template_properties.overview: Harbormaster of Saltmere Port. A broad-shouldered woman in her late forties (in Kaelen's era; late sixties by Aric's era) with weather-roughened skin, gray-streaked black hair tied back in a sailor's knot, and the still, measuring gaze of someone who has watched a harbor for long enough to read its moods at a glance. Cael keeps the port's logs, arbitrates dock disputes, signs or withholds mooring rights, and in her official capacity represents Saltmere's guild council to visitors. In her unofficial capacity — which matters more by the son's era — she quietly runs what remains of the port's honest trade, and an increasing amount of its dishonest trade, because the Vanguard's tightening grip on coastal commerce has made the distinction meaningless.
template_properties.backstory: Cael was born in Saltmere to a family of coastal traders and apprenticed on her mother's two-masted cutter before she was ten. By twenty she captained her own boat; by thirty she sat on the guild council; at thirty-eight she was appointed harbormaster after her predecessor died in a storm, and she has held the post ever since. In Kaelen's era, she is ten years into the job and still has her predecessor's desk, his kettle, and his rules. She cooperated easily with the old Vanguard on patrol coordination and dock inspections. A year before Aric's quest begins, the current Vanguard command tried to install a liaison officer in her tower over her objection. She refused the order, received a formal reprimand, and has been officially on administrative probation ever since — which she ignores. Her son went out on a fishing boat two years ago and did not come back; the boat was found a week later with a crystalline film on its hull and no crew. She has said nothing publicly about this.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Cael is economical in speech and direct in her dealings. She does not flatter, apologize, or explain herself unless the explanation is useful to the person she is speaking with. She has a dry, quiet humor that surfaces with people she likes. With strangers, she is cordial and gives nothing away. She reads people by their hands first, their eyes second, and their words last. Her primary motivation is keeping Saltmere functional — feeding its people, keeping its boats in the water, maintaining the harbor as something worth being harbormaster of. Her deeper motivation, which she has not spoken aloud since her son's boat was found, is finding out what took him and whether it is the same thing that is silting up the shallows. She suspects it is. She has not forgiven anyone for it, including herself.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A broad-shouldered woman, salt-weathered skin, black hair shot through with gray and tied in a tight sailor's knot at the base of her neck. Deep-set eyes with a measuring gaze. Wears a navy blue harbormaster's coat over a heavy cream linen shirt, brass buttons tarnished green-black, a wide leather belt with a ring of keys and a small brass spyglass hanging from it. Hands visible, callused, resting on a log book. In the father's era: shorter hair, the coat newer, fewer lines at the eyes. In the son's era: the hair is gray, the coat patched at the shoulder, and there is a small silver pin on the lapel — a fishing boat in profile — that she has worn since her son's boat was found. Cold gray-blue overcast lighting, suggesting the perpetual harbor weather.
creative_prompts.voice: Low contralto with a smoker's rasp (she does not smoke; that is just her voice). Measured cadence. She chooses her words and will sometimes stop mid-sentence if she decides the sentence is not worth finishing. No regional accent — the harbormaster trains one out — but a faint sailor's rhythm in how she breathes. Her laugh is rare, short, and genuine when it happens. When she is angry, she speaks more slowly, not faster. When she is grieving, she speaks about ropes, timbers, and tides as if they were the thing that mattered.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo bowed double bass in B minor, slow, with a low-register melody that rises and falls like the tide line on a dock post. A pennywhistle enters in the second phrase with a counter-melody in a higher register — not the concertina of the port's public theme, but a thin, lonely whistle like a boatswain's pipe heard across water. The piece does not resolve; it ends on an unresolved fifth that waits. In the son's era, the pennywhistle drops out entirely and is replaced by long silences between bass phrases. The listener waits for the whistle to come back. It does not.

dialogue_hooks: [[Saltmere Port]] — her town and her jurisdiction; she refers to it as "the harbor" rather than "the town"
[[Kaelen]] — in father's era, she knows him by reputation (Thornwall's hero-militia-man) but has never met; "Iron in the Tide" is their first meeting
[[Aric]] — in son's era, she recognizes Kaelen's son at a glance and does not say so; she is still deciding whether to help him
[[Brine]] — the Saltscale Inn keeper, one of the three people in the port she actually trusts
[[Nella]] — dockside fishmonger; Cael buys her breakfast from Nella every day and has done so for twenty-five years

template_properties.overview: A figure Jessa Vale sees on the Lastwatch signal platform at irregular intervals in the son's era — always at dusk, always at the southwest edge of the platform, always looking at the Iron Peaks. He wears the joint-militia coat of a keep watchman from roughly thirty years ago, and his right shoulder and neck are fused with crystalline growth that extends down into the stone of the platform itself, as if he is part of the keep's fabric. He is not clearly alive, not clearly dead, and not clearly present. He does not speak. He does not appear to notice Jessa. When she approaches him, he is no longer there.
template_properties.backstory: The watchman appears to be Tesh Hallam, a keep watchman who disappeared in a rockfall near Lastwatch forty years ago — the official record has him as killed-in-service, body unrecovered. Tesh was a young man at the time, serving his first posting; he had been at the keep for four months. What his appearance on the platform means is unclear. Jessa, who has kept the only continuous journal of these sightings, believes he began appearing roughly two years after the crystal corruption reached the keep's foundations. Whether he is a ghost, a memory the crystal is replaying, a warning, or something the keep itself is growing, she has not decided. She has sixty-three entries.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: He has no personality that can be observed. He has no apparent motivation. Jessa notes, carefully, that he always looks south-southwest — toward the Iron Peaks' sealed caverns, and toward Ashenford beyond them. She notes, also carefully, that he appears only at dusk and only when the signal fire is being lit for the evening. She has twice seen him turn his head, very slowly, and look directly at the signal flame. The flame flickers when he does. Whether he is responding to the flame or whether the flame is responding to him, she has not decided.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A young-looking man — early twenties in apparent age — wearing a dark blue joint-militia watchman's coat (the old cooperative design, three-flames sigil on the collar). His face is pale and composed, with no expression, looking away from the viewer to the south-southwest. His right shoulder and the right side of his neck are a pale violet crystalline growth that does not end at his skin — it extends down into the stone of the signal platform beneath him, fusing watchman and keep. His left hand rests on a signal-horn that is clearly the same signal-horn Jessa uses — or its copy. Dusk lighting, cold silver-blue, with a small warm point of orange from the signal fire at the edge of the frame. He does not cast a clear shadow.
creative_prompts.voice: He has no voice in the fiction. He has never spoken in any of Jessa's sixty-three entries. When he is approached, he is not there.
creative_prompts.theme-music: A single pitched crystal tone — not quite a note, closer to a sustained pure-tone oscillator — held for eight slow seconds before a second tone joins it a perfect fifth above. A third tone slowly phases in a quarter-tone off from the fifth, creating a faint audible beat. Underneath all of this, so quiet it may be imagined, a fragment of a joint-militia march that no one has played in thirty years — played backwards, very slowly, as if recalled rather than remembered. The piece has no rhythm. It has no resolution. It fades, slowly, over twelve seconds, leaving the listener unsure whether it was ever playing.

dialogue_hooks: [[Lastwatch]] — the keep, to which he appears to be physically attached
[[Jessa Vale]] — the warden who documents him; she says good evening to him, as a courtesy, when she lights the signal fire
[[The Crystal Shadow]] — in Aric's quest, Aric sees the watchman once and Jessa tells him what she knows; the watchman does not acknowledge either of them

template_properties.overview: Warden of Lastwatch, the stone keep between the Iron Peaks and Starlight Academy. Jessa is a tall, spare woman with prematurely gray hair that she keeps cropped short above her ears, sharp cheekbones, and eyes the pale gray of a winter sky. She took the warden's post at twenty-two and has not left the keep since. In Kaelen's era, she is in her early thirties and has been warden for ten years — a respected figure among the joint-militia council that commissioned the keep, with a reputation for clean signal discipline and clear-eyed reporting. In Aric's era, she is fifty-two and has been warden for thirty years; she is the only living soul in a keep that has been partially consumed by crystal growth from the rock below, and no one from any faction has visited her in six years. She has kept the signal fire lit the whole time.
template_properties.backstory: Jessa was born in a coastal militia town and named for her grandmother Hesper Vale, the warden who gave Lastwatch its last famous wrong signal fifty years ago. That signal — sent one winter night during a rockslide — reported an enemy raid that was not happening, and by the time the militias arrived at the keep, three of Hesper's own had died trying to correct the signal before reinforcements wasted a night's march. Hesper was not formally punished; the joint-militia council ruled the signal "an error of observation in poor visibility" and carved her name into the courtyard stone. The stone, and the shame of it, shaped Jessa's childhood. She enlisted in the coastal militia at sixteen with the explicit intention of earning a Lastwatch posting, trained in signal discipline and mountain observation, and received her warden's commission at twenty-two — the youngest warden in the keep's history. Six years before Aric's quest, when the joint-militia council dissolved under the Vanguard's new doctrine and the other factions ceased supporting the keep, Jessa was ordered to abandon her post. She acknowledged the order, thanked the council, and did not leave. No one has come to remove her.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Jessa is precise, patient, and not unkind. She speaks sparingly, but she answers direct questions directly. She has the composure of someone who has lived alone with her own thoughts long enough to have finished most of the arguments. She will offer a visitor tea from her tower hearth without comment on who they are or why they have come; the offer is simply what one does when the wind is cold. Her primary motivation is keeping the signal fire lit, the logs current, and the keep's one remaining function intact — Lastwatch was built to watch the passes, and she watches them. Her deeper motivation, which she has never spoken aloud to anyone, is that she believes, without evidence she can articulate, that there will come a night when the signal will matter again and that her grandmother's name will be answered for. She has not been right about this so far. She is also not going to be wrong first.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A tall, spare woman with short-cropped gray hair and sharp cheekbones. Pale gray eyes, steady gaze. In the father's era: mid-thirties, wears a warden's wool coat in the dark blue of the joint-militia (older, cooperative sigil on the collar — a triangle of three flames for the three commissioning factions), a leather belt with a signaling-horn and a small iron pulley key. Her hands are strong and lined from rope work. In the son's era: early fifties, the same coat now patched at the elbows and lighter gray at the shoulders from thirty years of sun through the tower window, the cooperative sigil still on the collar though the council that wore it has dissolved. Her hands are the same. Cold silver-blue lighting from a high window in both versions; Jessa is lit as if the signal fire above were always just out of frame.
creative_prompts.voice: Clear mezzo-soprano with a precise, unhurried cadence. She does not fill silences. Her sentences are complete and short. When she tells a visitor what to do, she does so as a statement of fact ("The tea is to your left. The wind will drop at dusk."), not as a command. She has a small, private laugh — more a breath than a sound — that she uses when someone says something accidentally true. No regional accent; thirty years of solitary logs have flattened it. When she speaks of her grandmother, her voice goes slower rather than softer, as if she were reading aloud from an older book.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo violin in A minor, played high on the instrument's register, with a clean unwavering tone. The melody is a long line that rises gradually across sixteen bars and descends in a shorter phrase, then repeats — the shape of a warden's scan across the horizon and back. A low sustained drone on a bowed psaltery sits beneath, unchanging. In the father's era, a second violin enters quietly in the second half and plays in thirds with the first — cooperative counterpoint, a reminder that the keep was made by multiple hands. In the son's era, the second violin is gone. The first violin plays the same line, alone, at the same unwavering tone. What has been lost is not in the melody. It is in everything the melody used to share the air with.

dialogue_hooks: [[Lastwatch]] — her post for thirty years; she refers to it as "the keep" and never as "home," though it has been both
[[Iron Peaks]] — the mountains she watches from the signal platform; she knows their rockfall patterns better than any living person

[[Starlight Academy]] — one of the three factions that commissioned the keep; she once sent a signal that saved a research expedition, and the Academy used to send her a copy of every major star-chart they produced as courtesy — the last one arrived six years ago

[[Kaelen]] — in the father's era, he passes through the keep during his Iron Peaks mission; she gives him tea, exchanges twelve sentences with him, and signals his departure to Ashenford and the Academy per protocol
[[Aric]] — in the son's era, he finds her still at her post, and she recognizes his father's line before he introduces himself

template_properties.overview: The Father. A wandering swordsman who became a legendary hero through a combination of genuine courage and lucky timing. Kaelen travels the land resolving crises — clearing monster nests, mediating faction disputes, protecting trade routes. The world remembers him as a paragon. The truth is more complicated: Kaelen is impulsive, self-doubting, and terrified of the growing darkness he cannot name. He left his infant son Aric in the care of Thornwall's elder to pursue what he believes is a threat too dangerous to face with a family at his back.
template_properties.backstory: Kaelen grew up in Thornwall, a fortified farming village at the foot of the Iron Peaks. His father was a militia guard; his mother was a traveling herbalist who rarely came home. He learned swordsmanship from the village militia and survival from the forest rangers. At nineteen, he drove off a band of raiders attacking Thornwall's granary — his first act of heroism, though he later admitted he was simply too scared to run. Word spread, and over the next decade he became the region's go-to problem-solver. He married a healer named Lira, had a son (Aric), and tried to settle down. But strange tremors from the sealed caverns beneath the Iron Peaks convinced him something ancient was stirring. He left Thornwall when Aric was two years old, promising to return within a year. He never did.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Kaelen is warm, direct, and physically brave — the kind of person who enters a burning building without thinking. He is also emotionally avoidant: he jokes when he should grieve, fights when he should talk, and leaves when he should stay. His core motivation is protecting people from a threat he doesn't fully understand. His deeper, unspoken motivation is proving he's worthy of the legend others have built around him. He fears that if he stops moving, the gap between who he is and who people think he is will become visible.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Man in his early thirties, strong jaw, short brown hair with a few premature gray streaks. Wears practical traveling armor — leather over chainmail, a worn green cloak fastened with a simple iron clasp. A broadsword strapped to his back. Expression is confident but weary — the look of someone who has been walking for a long time. Warm golden campfire lighting from below, casting upward shadows. Scars on his left forearm visible.
creative_prompts.voice: Mid-range tenor with a rough, outdoor quality. Speaks quickly when excited, slows down when trying to sound authoritative. Laughs easily — a genuine, disarming laugh that makes people trust him. When lying or deflecting, his pitch rises slightly. Regional accent: slightly clipped consonants, open vowels, the speech patterns of a farming village. Swears casually and without malice.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Acoustic guitar melody in G major, fingerpicked with a walking rhythm. Bright and forward-moving, suggesting open roads and purpose. A tin whistle joins in the chorus, adding a folk quality. Underneath, a low drone on a hurdy-gurdy provides grounding weight. The piece has momentum — it sounds like someone who can't sit still. Key modulates to E minor in the bridge, hinting at the cost of always moving forward.

dialogue_hooks: [[Aric]] — his son, left behind in Thornwall at age two
[[Thornwall]] — his home village, where his reputation began
[[Elder Aldric]] — consulted the elder about the star-charts before heading north
[[Iron Vanguard]] — the military faction that both helps and hinders his journey

template_properties.overview: Master armorer of Lastwatch in the father's era. A broad, grizzled man in his fifties with iron-gray hair cropped short, forearms ridged with old hammer scars, and a habit of tapping the steel he is working with his knuckle to hear its tone. Holt has kept the keep's armory for twenty years — sharpening blades, refitting scale, rehafting tools for the four-man garrison and the warden, and maintaining the signal platform's pulley system from the inside. He is the keep's second most permanent fixture after Jessa Vale. He does not appear in the son's era; the armory is sealed behind crystal growth.
template_properties.backstory: Holt learned his trade in a coastal foundry town and enlisted in the joint militia at twenty-five after a falling-out with his brother over the family forge. He served in three postings before requesting Lastwatch at thirty-five, and once he arrived, he did not request another. He is not married. He has two grown children he writes to monthly; their mother is a widow who runs an inn in a coastal plain village he visits once a year on leave. He believes — though he does not say so — that the joint militia's cooperative model is the finest institution he has ever belonged to, and that Lastwatch is its finest outpost.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Steady, practical, and warm in a gruff way. Holt greets travelers at the armory door with a quick assessment of their gear and an offer to fix whatever is broken in exchange for a reasonable fee — or nothing, if the traveler is obviously passing through on militia business. His primary motivation is keeping the garrison's kit in working order; his deeper motivation is the signal pulley. He has rebuilt it three times over twenty years and considers it his quiet contribution to whatever the keep is watching for. The day Kaelen passes through, Holt offers to sharpen Iron Dawn for free, as a courtesy. Kaelen accepts.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A broad man in his fifties with iron-gray hair clipped short and a heavy gray-black beard. Leather apron over a short-sleeved cream linen shirt, forearms bare and lined with pale hammer-scars. A heavy leather belt with a small hammer in a loop at the hip. Sooty hands resting on the shaft of a half-finished blade. Warm orange forge-light from behind him lights the edges of his silhouette; the rest of the portrait is in cooler stone-gray from the armory's north window.
creative_prompts.voice: Deep bass with a slight rumble, unhurried cadence. Has a habit of tapping on what he is talking about — a blade, a table, a pulley rope — while he explains it, the way a good teacher underlines words. Laughs loud and short. Swears rarely and creatively, always about inanimate objects, never about people.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Hammer-on-anvil rhythm in 4/4, opening with four strikes alone before a low wooden flute enters with a simple melody in D major — direct, warm, without ornamentation. A second hammer adds a counter-rhythm at the halfway mark. The piece is a craftsman's march: regular, confident, grounded in work. It ends on a single final hammer-strike at the end of the sixteenth bar.

dialogue_hooks: [[Lastwatch]] — the keep and the armory; his quiet domain
[[Jessa Vale]] — the warden he has served with for ten years; they take tea together every seventh day, a ritual he invented
[[The Watch Holds]] — in this quest, he assists Kaelen with preparations and repairs for the cavern-breach mission

template_properties.overview: Aric's childhood friend and Thornwall's best tracker. Maren is a wiry, freckled young woman who can read animal trails the way Aldric reads star charts. She is practical, blunt, and fiercely protective of the people she cares about. Unlike Aric, she has no interest in Kaelen's legacy — she cares about the here and now, and the here and now is that the forest is dying and the village needs help. She joins Aric not because of destiny or duty but because someone has to keep him from getting killed.
template_properties.backstory: Maren's parents were foresters who managed the woodlands north of Thornwall. She grew up in the canopy, learning to track, trap, and navigate by moss patterns and bird calls. When the forest began changing — trees rotting from the inside, animal migrations shifting — her parents were among the first to notice. Her father died investigating a sinkhole that opened in the deep woods when Maren was seventeen. Her mother now runs the village's dwindling herb supply. Maren took over forest patrols alone, mapping the decay and warning Thornwall's council, who largely ignored her reports until creatures began emerging from the cracks.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Direct to the point of bluntness, Maren has no patience for abstractions or philosophical debates. She communicates through action and expects the same from others. She is not unkind — she shows affection through practical gestures: mending gear, sharing rations, covering someone's blind spot in a fight. Her primary motivation is protecting Thornwall's remaining woodlands and her mother. Her deeper motivation is proving that the people who ignored her warnings were wrong — and making sure no one else dies the way her father did.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Young woman in her early twenties, wiry build, freckled face, reddish-brown hair in a messy braid. Wears forest-practical clothing — muted green tunic, leather arm guards, a quiver of short arrows on her back. A hand-carved wooden whistle hangs from her neck (used for bird calls). Expression is alert and slightly impatient — she looks like she'd rather be outside. Natural dappled forest lighting, green-filtered sunlight through leaves.
creative_prompts.voice: Clear alto with a brisk cadence. Speaks without filler words — no "uh" or "well." Her sentences are short and declarative. When frustrated, she talks faster rather than louder. Has a habit of clicking her tongue when thinking. Warm tone when speaking to animals or children, sharper with adults she considers wasting time. Slight whistle on sibilants from a chipped front tooth.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Quick-tempo fiddle melody in A major, accompanied by a hand drum. Bright, percussive, grounded — the music of someone who moves through the world on foot. The fiddle line is syncopated, suggesting agility and unpredictability. A low wooden flute provides countermelody, evoking forest sounds. The piece is short and loops cleanly, like a walking rhythm. No grand crescendo — just steady, reliable momentum.

dialogue_hooks: [[Aric]] — childhood friend, the only person who took her forest reports seriously
[[Thornwall]] — home, the village she patrols for

template_properties.overview: Dockside fishmonger at the foot of the Low Docks in Saltmere Port. A compact, weather-beaten woman in her fifties (seventies by Aric's era), always in the same oilcloth coat and rope-belted apron, Nella has sold dockside fish out of the same wooden stall for thirty years and, in the process, has become the port's unofficial rumor source. She knows what every boat brought in before the harbormaster's log is written. She knows who has not paid their mooring fee in three weeks and why. She has strong opinions about every captain in the harbor and she shares them freely with anyone buying breakfast.
template_properties.backstory: Nella inherited the stall from her mother, who inherited it from her mother. The Low Docks fishmonger role has been in Nella's family for four generations and is not formally part of the port's guild charter — it is older than the charter and nobody has ever dared to try to regularize it. Nella is twice widowed, has three grown children who all left Saltmere for the coastal plain, and lives in two rooms above the stall's storage shed with a one-eyed cat named Ship. In the son's era, she sells far less fish (the catch has thinned), but she is still at the stall every dawn.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Sharp-tongued and warm-hearted in equal measure, Nella speaks her mind whether asked or not and considers it an act of generosity. She will answer a stranger's question with a question, size them up, and then decide how much of the truth to give them. Her primary motivation is keeping the stall running and Ship fed. Her deeper motivation is that she believes Saltmere's working-class are the port's actual backbone — not the guild, not the harbormaster, not the Vanguard — and she has made herself a small quiet center of that backbone by being where they buy their breakfast.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Compact, weather-beaten woman with salt-gray hair tied under a blue kerchief. Squinting eyes from thirty years of dawn sun on water. Wears a black oilcloth coat over a rope-belted apron stained with fish scales and brine. Hands constantly in motion — gutting, scaling, wrapping. A small knife worn at the hip in a leather sheath, the handle worn smooth and pale by her thumb.
creative_prompts.voice: Sharp alto, brisk cadence, a chopped-off way of ending sentences that suggests she does not waste breath. Coastal accent that she does not hide. Laughs at her own jokes — which are often at the expense of captains, guild officers, and anyone wearing new boots. Has a habit of calling strangers "love" and regulars by their names; the distinction matters.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Hand drum and tin whistle in D minor, quick tempo, played as if by a street-corner busker. The melody is a simple street-cry set to music — the sound of a working waterfront. The piece is deliberately rough, with uneven phrasing and a drum hit that occasionally lands a fraction late. It is the sound of a stall that has been in the same spot since before anyone alive can remember.

dialogue_hooks: [[Saltmere Port]] — the port she has watched from one stall for thirty years
[[Cael Vire]] — Cael has bought her breakfast from Nella every day for twenty-five years; neither woman would describe this as friendship and both would be offended if the other ever did

template_properties.overview: Herbalist and physician of Hollow's Rest. A slight woman of indeterminate middle age with sun-browned skin, graying dark hair in a loose braid, and hands that are never still — always sorting, grinding, measuring, folding paper envelopes of dried herb. Wren came to Hollow's Rest three months before Aric arrives, from a collapsed coastal village that she does not discuss. She is the settlement's only trained physician, and the only person who seems to know which of the new, corruption-adjacent plant growths in the Whispering Woods are safe to use medicinally and which will kill you slowly.
template_properties.backstory: Wren trained at a small physician's school on the coast — not the Starlight Academy, a smaller institution that did not survive the son's-era collapses — and spent her working life treating fishermen, farmers, and their children in a village north of Saltmere that lost two-thirds of its population in a single summer when a wave of crystal-adjacent illness swept through. She was the last physician standing when the village emptied out. She walked three weeks through the Whispering Woods with a pack of her most valuable books and one child — her apprentice, ten years old — and found Hollow's Rest by following a trail Torbin had left deliberately for people in her condition. The child apprentice is now fourteen and rarely leaves Wren's side.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Focused and gentle, with a dry quiet humor that emerges only when she is tired. Wren treats her patients one at a time, with full attention, and she does not hurry. She is brisk with malingerers and infinitely patient with the genuinely sick. Her primary motivation is keeping Hollow's Rest's people alive through the winters and the corruption-adjacent illnesses that have started to appear. Her deeper motivation is teaching her apprentice everything she knows while there is still time, because she has seen what happens when the last physician leaves without teaching anyone.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A slight woman of indeterminate middle age, sun-browned skin, graying dark hair in a loose braid over one shoulder. A linen physician's apron over a wool dress, both in forest greens and browns with small herb stains. A leather belt with small pouches, each carefully labeled with stitched symbols. Reading spectacles on a cord. A small silver pin on her collar shaped like a sprig of some plant that does not quite exist. Hands always in motion — the portrait catches her mid-grinding with a small mortar and pestle.
creative_prompts.voice: Soft contralto, patient cadence, a slight coastal lilt. Speaks in full sentences but will go silent for long stretches while working. Uses a patient's name frequently while treating them — "Breathe out, Torbin. Good." — as a grounding technique. Occasionally sings under her breath in a language no one at Hollow's Rest recognizes.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo hammered dulcimer in D minor, played at a walking tempo. The melody is repetitive in a way that feels deliberate — the sound of a medicine being prepared the same way it has been prepared for generations. A second voice enters mid-piece: a child's alto humming a harmonizing line, slightly uncertain, learning. This is the apprentice. The hammered dulcimer continues steadily while the child's humming finds and loses its way. The music ends with the two voices resolving to unison.

dialogue_hooks: [[Hollow's Rest]] — her home for three months and counting; she has started calling it that
[[Torbin]] — the elder who welcomed her; they drink tea together once a week and discuss nothing in particular
[[Whispering Woods]] — the source of her materia medica and, increasingly, her materia pathologica; she maps the corruption's advance through her plant stocks

template_properties.overview: Quartermaster of the Ashenford Vanguard depot. A short, stocky man with a face like a disappointed pillow and a voice that has ground itself thin on fifteen years of daily inventory. Berin controls the flow of supplies into and out of Ashenford — food, medicine, weapons, cloth, tools — and in Kaelen's era he serves as the primary point of contact for civilian travelers who need to purchase Vanguard-released goods at the lowland rate. In Aric's era, he still holds the post, but his inventory is larger, his disbursements are smaller, and his temper is worse.
template_properties.backstory: Berin enlisted in the Vanguard at nineteen because his family's coastal-plain farm could not support another son, and he discovered, to his own surprise, that he had an aptitude for accounts. He rose through the quartermaster corps and arrived at Ashenford as a subordinate clerk under Rhen Halloway. Within four years he was promoted to senior quartermaster. He served under Rhen for fifteen years and considered the captain the closest thing he had to a mentor. When Rhen turned in his commission six years before Aric's quest, Berin did not follow — he had a daughter to support and a pension to protect — and he has not forgiven himself for the choice.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Gruff, methodical, and quietly bitter. Berin treats everyone who comes to his depot with the same abrupt courtesy, civilian or soldier; the abruptness is procedural, not personal. He is scrupulously honest with his accounts and slightly less scrupulous with the paperwork around them — he has, on several occasions in the son's era, mis-filed a supply release in a way that let a refugee family receive something they would not otherwise have gotten. He does not call this generosity. He calls it "clerical error." His primary motivation is keeping the depot's books clean and its shelves stocked. His deeper motivation is private and connected to the captain who is not his captain anymore.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Short, stocky man in his late forties (mid-fifties in son's era). Pale blue Vanguard service coat with quartermaster's double-chevron on the sleeve, a heavy leather belt with an iron ring of inventory keys, a stub pencil tucked behind his ear. Thinning brown hair going gray. A face that looks mildly disappointed as its resting expression. Ink stains on his fingers.
creative_prompts.voice: Gravelly baritone that sounds like it has said "next" ten thousand times. Brisk cadence. Uses institutional phrasing by reflex ("requisition slip, three copies") but will slip into plain speech if the person in front of him seems worth the breath. Sighs before he says anything important, as if each important sentence costs him something.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Low brass (tuba or euphonium) in G minor, playing a slow, regular march-like pattern — the sound of an institution grinding on. A quiet tack-tack rhythm of a wooden pencil on a desk sits underneath, in steady 4/4 time. The melody is formal and repetitive, then briefly opens up in the middle for four bars into something warmer — a phrase that sounds borrowed from Rhen Halloway's theme — before returning to the institutional grind. Berin's music is Rhen's theme with a pencil on top of it.

dialogue_hooks: [[Ashenford]] — the depot and the town; one and the same in his mind
[[Iron Vanguard]] — the institution; he salutes the current banner without conviction
[[Rhen Halloway]] — his former captain; they have not spoken in six years and both of them count the days

template_properties.overview: Captain of the Ashenford garrison in the father's era, one of the last Iron Vanguard officers who served under the old cooperative doctrine before the militarization. Rhen is tall, lean, and precisely groomed in his Vanguard grays, with short iron-gray hair and a small scar at the corner of his mouth from an arrow graze he took escorting a salt caravan as a junior officer. In Kaelen's era he is forty-five, fifteen years into his Ashenford command, and widely respected by both his soldiers and the lowland villages that depend on the mountain road he keeps open. In Aric's era, Rhen is sixty-five and has been retired for six years; he still lives in Ashenford, in a small stone house at the edge of the refugee camp, and he has not worn a uniform since the day he turned in his commission in protest of the new doctrine.
template_properties.backstory: Rhen enlisted in the Vanguard at seventeen, coming out of a tenant-farmer family from the coastal plain. He rose through the ranks as a patrol officer, built a reputation for clean judgment and for refusing bribes from merchants who wanted to move unlisted cargo through the Peaks, and earned his captaincy at thirty. The Ashenford command came to him at thirty because the previous captain had died of a stroke and the Vanguard high command wanted someone who would keep the lowland councils comfortable — Rhen was that man. For the next fifteen years, he ran Ashenford as a cooperative garrison, attending village council meetings as often as parade drills, and kept the road to the Peaks open with a patrol system that was efficient without being oppressive. Six years before Aric's quest, after the deep-tunnel resonance incident that killed a patrol squad and changed the doctrine, Rhen was ordered to begin fortifying the canyon mouth into a permanent checkpoint and to cease cooperative agreements with the lowland councils. He refused. He was offered a transfer to a coastal posting. He refused that too. He turned in his commission on a cold morning in early spring and walked out of Ashenford's Garrison Square in civilian clothes, and he has not been back inside it since.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: In Kaelen's era, Rhen is calm, formal, and quietly warm — a professional man with a strong sense of obligation to both the institution he serves and the communities it was meant to serve. He speaks plainly and listens carefully. He has a subtle sense of humor that surfaces over shared meals and disappears during duty hours. His primary motivation is keeping the mountain road open for everyone who needs it — militia, miners, merchants, foresters — and he believes, with real conviction, that the Vanguard at its best is a service to the lowlands rather than a force over them. In Aric's era, Rhen is a broken and stubborn old man. He has not forgiven himself for not seeing the militarization coming, for the patrol squad that died underground, or for the lowland communities his refusal could not save. His motivation is smaller now: he helps, one person at a time, when people come to him. He does not lead anyone. He will not.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A tall, lean man with short iron-gray hair, clean-shaven, a small pale scar at the corner of his mouth. In the father's era: Vanguard dress grays — a wool coat with black iron buttons and a steel-and-red cooperative shoulder insignia (the old doctrine's sigil), a polished leather belt, a short sword at his hip. His posture is straight without being rigid. His eyes are steady, slightly tired. In the son's era: the same man at sixty-five, wearing a plain civilian coat of undyed wool over a linen shirt, no belt, no sword, no insignia. The hair is fully silver. The posture is slightly stooped at the shoulders — not from age, from something heavier. The eyes are the same. Cold gray overcast lighting in both versions; Rhen does not look right in warm light.
creative_prompts.voice: Clear baritone with precise diction and a faint coastal-plain cadence that he has never fully trained out. Speaks in complete sentences, often with a brief pause before the final clause while he chooses between two words. Has a distinctive habit of repeating a question back before answering it ("Why the road. Because the road is the town's blood. Anything else?"). In the father's era, his voice carries institutional authority without shouting; he never raises his voice because he does not need to. In the son's era, his voice is softer and slower — not weaker, just quieter. He still pauses before the final clause. He still chooses between two words.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo trumpet in D minor, muted (with a Harmon mute or equivalent), played at the tempo of a slow formal march. The melodic line is dignified and slightly melancholy, built from short phrases separated by deliberate rests — the musical equivalent of Rhen's speech pattern. A low string drone provides the ground. In the father's era, a snare drum enters in the second half of the piece, unmuted, playing a slow cadence underneath the trumpet — the institution still present behind the man. In the son's era, the snare drum is gone entirely; the trumpet plays alone, and the drone beneath it has thinned to a single sustained note. The piece is the same. What has been taken away is everything around it.

dialogue_hooks: [[Ashenford]] — the town he commanded for fifteen years and now lives in as a private citizen at its edge
[[Iron Vanguard]] — the institution he served for twenty-eight years and will not speak ill of, even now
[[Kaelen]] — in father's era, a visiting militia fighter he grants passage to in "The Vanguard's Debt"; they will meet once more
[[Aric]] — in son's era, the son of the man he remembers; Aric finds him on the porch of the small stone house
[[Quartermaster Berin]] — his depot quartermaster of fifteen years; they have not spoken since Rhen turned in his commission

template_properties.overview: Hollow's Rest's supply runner. A wiry young man in his mid-twenties with reddish hair cut unevenly short, a long face, and a permanent smudge of road-dust across the bridge of his nose. Mott makes the three-day round trip out of the settlement roughly once every two weeks, salvaging what he can from abandoned farmsteads, trading quietly with Nella at Saltmere when a signal is right, and occasionally palming something off a Vanguard supply cart when the quartermaster looks the other way. He does not steal from people. He steals from institutions. He is very particular about the distinction.
template_properties.backstory: Mott was born in Thornwall; his family was one of the seventeen that followed Torbin into the woods three years ago. He was twenty-two at the time. Of the five young adults who came with their families, he was the only one who volunteered to go back out on the roads. He has a knack for reading which farmsteads have been visited by the Vanguard and which have been forgotten, which signals mean a trader is willing to deal and which mean a trap, and which parts of the forest are safe to cross at which hours. He has been caught twice by the Vanguard. Both times he has talked his way out. He does not tell Torbin about the second time.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Fast-talking, quick-moving, and genuinely cheerful in a way that surprises people who expect a scavenger to be grim. Mott likes his work — not the stealing, but the knowing — and he likes bringing things back. He tells stories at the Hearth Circle after a successful run and edits the scary parts out. His primary motivation is keeping Hollow's Rest supplied; his deeper motivation is being useful in a way that proves (to himself, never out loud) that the young adults who came with their families made the right choice to follow the old man into the woods.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A wiry young man in his mid-twenties. Red-brown hair cut uneven by his own hand; a long thin face with freckles scattered across the nose. Wears layered road clothes — a faded green tunic over a canvas shirt, brown breeches, well-patched knee-high leather boots. A light canvas pack on one shoulder with the flap loose for quick access. A small curved knife at his belt, a water skin, and a hand-bound notebook in a side pocket where he logs everything he brings back. A smudge of road-dust across his nose that is basically permanent.
creative_prompts.voice: Quick tenor, musical cadence, full of warmth. Talks with his hands; you can hear it even when you cannot see him. Uses road-trader's slang freely and explains it if asked. Tells stories well. Lies smoothly when necessary and is aware, always, that he is doing it. His real voice emerges at the Hearth Circle at night, when he is unwinding — it is slower and quieter than his road voice.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Fingerpicked acoustic guitar in G major, quick tempo, bright phrasing. The melody is a traveling song — the kind of tune a road-trader hums to keep pace. Hand drum enters in the second pass, adding a light driving rhythm. The piece is confident and forward-moving, but in the bridge, briefly, the key shifts to E minor and the drum drops out, leaving the guitar alone to play four quiet measures — the sound of Mott between Ashenford and the woods, when no one is watching. Then the drum comes back in and the major key returns. This is his road voice and his Hearth Circle voice in a single piece.

dialogue_hooks: [[Hollow's Rest]] — home, the only place he has ever chosen to come back to
[[Torbin]] — the elder he lies to, gently, to keep him from worrying
[[Nella]] — Saltmere Port fishmonger; one of his quiet trade contacts; she calls him "redhead" and never uses his name
[[Ashenford]] — the Vanguard depot he steals from; he respects Berin without knowing why, and has, once, put a stolen supply back

template_properties.overview: A young scholar from the Academy of Starlight.
template_properties.backstory: Sera grew up in the shadow of the Academy.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Curious, cautious, fiercely loyal to her mentor.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. Young woman with short dark hair, wearing scholar robes. Expression is thoughtful, slightly guarded. Warm amber lighting from a desk lamp.
creative_prompts.voice: Soft-spoken, measured cadence, slight tremor when discussing the Academy. Mid-range pitch, educated vocabulary but not pretentious.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Melancholic piano melody in A minor, builds from single notes to gentle chords. Evokes studying alone in a vast library at night.

dialogue_hooks: [[Elder Aldric]] — her mentor at the Academy
[[Starlight Academy]] — her faction

template_properties.overview: Founder and elder of Hollow's Rest, the hidden refugee settlement in the deep Whispering Woods. Torbin is a small, thin man in his mid-seventies, with close-cropped white hair, a weathered beard clipped short, and hands that have spent sixty years working timber, rope, and accounts. He served on Thornwall's village council for three decades as the junior member who kept the ledgers and drafted the official correspondence with the Vanguard and the coastal guilds. In the son's era, three years before Aric's quest begins, Torbin led seventeen families out of Thornwall and into the woods when the Vanguard checkpoint made free movement impossible and the village council refused to formally acknowledge what was happening to the land. He is not the strongest person in Hollow's Rest, or the most skilled, or the most charismatic. He is the person who said out loud what the rest would not.
template_properties.backstory: Torbin was born in Thornwall and has never lived anywhere else until Hollow's Rest. He apprenticed with the village's woodwright as a young man, took over the workshop at thirty-five when his master retired, and was elected to the village council at forty for his careful record-keeping rather than any charisma. For thirty years, he drafted Thornwall's minutes, kept its tithe ledger, and corresponded in his small careful handwriting with every faction that had business with the village. He was married for forty-four years to a woman named Elen who taught the Thornwall children their letters; she died of a winter fever four years before Aric's quest. It was her death and the council's refusal, in the same season, to formally record that the northern farms had become uninhabitable that broke something in Torbin. He resigned from the council in the spring, spent the summer gathering the families who would listen to him, and walked into the woods on the first cold morning of autumn with seventeen households behind him.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Torbin is quiet, patient, and precise. He is not a talker; he listens. When he speaks in council — he still runs a council of sorts at Hollow's Rest, though it has no official charter — he waits until others have said what they needed to say, and then he summarizes what was said, fairly, and asks what the settlement should do. He has a slight tremor in his left hand that he controls by pressing it against his knee under the table. His primary motivation is keeping the fifty-plus people of Hollow's Rest alive, fed, and hidden — one winter at a time. His deeper motivation, which he has never named aloud, is that he was not brave enough to leave Thornwall while Elen was still alive, and he is determined not to be that kind of man again. He keeps her comb on his writing desk. He has not opened the sealed leather tube Kaelen left in the Thornwall ledger thirty years ago, and he knows he should.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A small, thin old man with close-cropped white hair and a short clipped beard. Deep-set eyes, heavy lids, a long patient face with pronounced laugh lines at the mouth that have become neutral with age. Wears layered wool and linen in forest colors — a moss-green overcoat of patched homespun, a cream tunic underneath, trousers of undyed wool, leather shoes that have been resoled more than once. A pair of small brass reading spectacles hangs from a cord around his neck. His left hand rests against his knee; his right holds a wooden writing stylus. Expression is calm and attentive. Dappled green forest lighting — Torbin is always rendered in the filtered light of Hollow's Rest's intact canopy.
creative_prompts.voice: Quiet tenor, slightly dry from years of speaking only when necessary. Deliberate cadence. He begins most responses with a pause that lasts just a beat longer than feels comfortable — he is genuinely thinking, not performing consideration. He says "I think" rather than "I know" even when he knows. He uses the word "we" almost always; "I" is rare enough that when he uses it, the listener notices. No regional accent; his council training trained it out. When he is moved, his voice goes softer rather than louder, and he looks at his hands.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Solo wooden recorder in E minor, played at a walking tempo with no accompaniment. The melody is a folk line — the kind of tune a man might hum while tallying a ledger — unadorned, simple, and quietly repeating. It has the shape of a song that was originally a children's lullaby; listeners who have heard Thornwall's Market theme in the father's era will hear a phrase from that theme buried in the middle of Torbin's piece, transposed down and slowed. A second voice enters briefly in the second pass — an alto humming without words, half a measure behind the recorder, following it. This is Elen. She is not in the fiction; she is in the music. The recorder continues after the humming fades.

dialogue_hooks: [[Hollow's Rest]] — the settlement he founded and continues to shepherd
[[Thornwall]] — his birthplace and the village he resigned from; he still considers himself a citizen in some private way
[[Aric]] — Torbin knew Aric as a child, taught him his letters alongside Elen, and recognizes him instantly
[[Kaelen]] — Torbin corresponded with him twice before he left for the Iron Peaks thirty years ago; he has been holding one of Kaelen's letters since
[[Maren]] — Maren's father was the forester who drew the maps that led Torbin to the Great Fall; Torbin holds Maren's father's work in something close to reverence

template_properties.overview: A Vanguard soldier — one of the few survivors of a patrol that went into the Iron Peaks six months before Aric's quest and returned, in pieces, over the course of three weeks. The sentry's right arm is crystalline from the elbow down: a pale violet growth that has fused with his flesh and taken the shape of an arm without being one. He cannot use it. It does not hurt. He can still stand watch. The Vanguard has kept him on active duty at the Ashenford checkpoint partly because he volunteers for the watches no one else will take and partly because the command does not quite know what else to do with him.
template_properties.backstory: The sentry was a junior corporal on the patrol that entered the sealed tunnels six months before Aric arrives. Six went in; two came back in the first week (raving, and died); one came back in the second (the sentry); three never came back at all. He does not remember what happened underground. He remembers going in with his squad. He remembers being alone on a slope above Ashenford with his right arm heavy and cold. He was carried into town by a Saltmere salt-trader who found him sitting on a rock and looking east toward the sea, saying nothing.
template_properties.personality_and_motivation: Quiet, calm, and unsettlingly present. The sentry speaks when spoken to, in short clear sentences, and volunteers nothing. He does not complain about his condition. He does not appear distressed by it. When he is alone on watch, he stares at the crystalline arm for long stretches; when someone approaches, he stops and looks at them. His primary motivation, as far as anyone can tell, is standing his watches. His deeper motivation, which he has not expressed to anyone, is that he has decided the arm is listening for something, and he is waiting to hear what it hears.

creative_prompts.portrait: 16-bit pixel art, grayscale base with palette-swap tinting. A young man in Vanguard gray service kit, standard steel half-plate. Short black hair, pale skin, a calm and slightly absent expression. Left side normal: gloved hand resting on the pommel of a standard-issue shortsword. Right side: the armor plate has been cut away below the shoulder, and from the elbow down his arm is a pale violet crystalline shape that roughly follows the original bone structure — a wrist, a hand, fingers — but is clearly mineral, faceted, and gives off a faint glow in low light. The crystalline arm is not grotesque; it is oddly beautiful, which is its own kind of wrong. Lighting is dim winter afternoon through the Ashenford canyon.
creative_prompts.voice: Light tenor, even volume, no inflection spikes. Speaks in short declarative sentences, often the minimum required to answer a question. Pauses before answering, not because he is thinking, but because he is listening to something else first. No tremor. No distress. The voice of someone who has decided that what happened to him is information rather than injury.
creative_prompts.theme-music: A high crystalline synthesizer tone in a key that doesn't quite fit anything else — F sharp — held as a drone underneath a solo flute melody that tries three times to resolve to a recognizable folk theme and fails each time. A distant snare drum cadence from the Ashenford garrison march is audible in the background, slightly out of time with the flute. The piece is short, doesn't loop cleanly, and leaves a small silence before restarting. The sentry's music is music that has learned not to finish its sentences.

dialogue_hooks: [[Ashenford]] — the garrison he serves; his barracks bunk has a crystal-shaped indent in the wall where his arm rests when he sleeps
[[Iron Vanguard]] — the institution that does not know what to do with him; he is polite to his officers
[[The Missing Patrol]] — the three soldiers who did not come back; he refers to them by rank and name, never by memory

template_properties.overview: A lowland town on the only maintained road between the coastal plain and the Iron Peaks, sited at the last flat ground before the canyon passes rise into the mountains. Ashenford grew up around a Vanguard garrison that predates the current mining crisis by generations. In Kaelen's era it is a functional military town — a garrison, a supply depot, stables, a farrier, and a handful of civilian trades serving soldiers and travelers passing through toward the Peaks. In Aric's era, Ashenford has been swallowed by the Vanguard's lockdown: the garrison expanded into a fortified checkpoint across the canyon mouth, the old civilian quarter has been half-absorbed into a military compound, and the rest has collapsed into a refugee camp for displaced miners and their families who cannot pass the checkpoint to return home.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Ashenford is built of gray ashlar stone and dark iron fittings, its buildings squat and functional rather than pretty. The main road runs straight through the town from the southern gate to the northern canyon mouth, with barracks, stables, and depot buildings flanking it. In Kaelen's era, the town feels ordered and busy — soldiers drilling in the yard, supply carts rolling north, a farrier's hammer ringing from the stable, Vanguard banners hung from iron brackets at regular intervals. The town is not welcoming, but it is professional and, if you have business, cooperative. In Aric's era, the same streets feel like a choke point. The Vanguard compound has grown, swallowing the old market and herb gardens. Refugee tents clot the southern edge of town in a makeshift camp. The banners have been replaced with newer ones in a harder gray; the old cooperative symbols are gone.
template_properties.history: Ashenford was founded a century and a half ago as a militia way-camp, when the Iron Peaks mines were still productive and the coastal towns paid cooperative tithes to maintain a force that kept the mountain passes safe from bandits and rockslides. Over generations, the militia professionalized into what became the Iron Vanguard, and the way-camp became a permanent garrison. In Kaelen's era, the Vanguard was still a regional force with cooperative obligations — its soldiers were trusted by the lowland communities, and its officers dined with village councils. After the deep-tunnel "resonance incident" that killed a patrol squad three years before Aric's quest, the Vanguard high command restructured under a new doctrine: geological containment as military priority. The Ashenford garrison was reinforced, the canyon mouth was fortified into a checkpoint, and the town's civilian life was gradually subordinated to the mission. The old tithe agreements lapsed. The lowland communities no longer speak of the Vanguard as partners.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Garrison Square — the parade ground at the heart of Ashenford, flanked by the barracks, the armory, and the command hall. In father's era: daily drill, a notice board with militia postings. In son's era: expanded with timber walls on three sides, now effectively a military staging yard.
- The Command Hall — a two-story stone building with an iron-banded door, housing the garrison commander's office. In father's era: Captain Rhen Halloway's desk faces a map of the lowlands with Vanguard patrol routes marked in red wax. In son's era: a different commander holds the post; Rhen Halloway's old office is sealed.
- The Depot — a long warehouse of stone and timber where Vanguard supplies are staged before shipment up into the Peaks. Quartermaster Berin runs it in both eras, though his temper has soured.
- The Traveler's Hearth — the town's only civilian inn in the father's era, on the south side of town. In the son's era, it has been repurposed as a refugee aid station run by displaced villagers.
- The Refugee Camp — in son's era only, a sprawl of canvas tents, lean-tos, and carted-out wagons clustered outside the southern gate. Home to miners and their families who fled the Peaks when the tunnels were sealed and cannot get home past the checkpoint.
- The Canyon Mouth — the northern edge of town, where the road narrows into the rocky pass that leads to the Iron Peaks. In father's era: a simple gatehouse and two sentry posts. In son's era: the full Vanguard Checkpoint from the quest of that name — timber-and-stone gatehouse, flanking watchtowers, iron-reinforced gates.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Militarized town tiles: gray ashlar stone building walls with dark iron fittings, cobblestone-and-dirt main road, timber-frame barracks with shuttered windows, stone-and-iron armory tiles, stable tiles with split doors and straw on the floor, a farrier's forge with glowing coals visible through an open wall. Father's era palette: warm gray stone, dark-stained timber, red-and-steel Vanguard banners, oil-lamp orange window light, worked earth tones. The town reads as busy-but-orderly — soldiers and civilians mixing in planned pedestrian flow. Son's era additions: expanded timber palisade tiles on three sides of the Garrison Square, refugee tent clusters (patched canvas, guy-ropes, small cook-fires) along the southern edge, a graffito-marked lapsed-banner tile on the old notice board, boarded-up civilian storefront tiles, trampled-mud tiles replacing the original paving near the canyon mouth. The old cooperative red-and-steel banners are replaced in son's era with a colder gray-on-gray Vanguard sigil.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: a military-working-town soundscape. Marching boots on flagstones in staggered patterns (a squad drilling distantly), the rhythmic ring of a farrier's hammer, horses snorting in the stables, a sergeant calling cadence, cartwheels over cobblestone, wood being stacked, the occasional laugh or shouted greeting between civilians and off-duty soldiers. Underneath: wind funneled down out of the canyon, a constant low flow that picks up at the northern edge of town. Son's era: the drill cadence is still there but fewer voices, and the civilians are absent from the central streets — replaced by the muted noise of the refugee camp at the southern edge (children, cooking, low conversations, the crack of canvas in the wind). The farrier's hammer still rings but the pattern is different — armoring work rather than horseshoeing. The canyon wind is unchanged. It was always loudest here. It is still loudest here. Add, in the son's era, the distant clang of the checkpoint gate opening and closing at unpredictable intervals — a sound the refugees flinch at.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: a march in E minor led by snare drum and low brass. Not menacing — professional. The snare cadence is crisp at a parade-step tempo (120 bpm), with a tenor horn carrying a straightforward melodic line that suggests duty done competently. A bass drum anchors on the one and three. The piece has a hummable middle passage on a solo fife that brings the military scaffolding down to human scale — the sound of a soldier off duty, whistling on his way home. Son's era: the same march cadence but stripped of the fife, and the brass line is transposed a minor third down, making it heavier and more oppressive. The snare drum now plays slightly behind the beat, a drag that suggests institutional fatigue. Beneath the march, a solo fiddle plays a folk melody from the refugee camp — a different song, in a different key, that never syncs with the march above it. The two musics coexist in the same space without agreeing.

template_properties.overview: A hidden settlement deep in the Whispering Woods, built in the hollow of a massive ancient tree-fall where three of the forest's oldest trunks toppled across one another generations ago. Hollow's Rest does not exist in Kaelen's era — the woods were too dense and too healthy to need a refuge, and the great fall had not yet formed. In Aric's era, as Thornwall and the smaller forest hamlets emptied under the tremors and the crystal corruption, a handful of families followed Torbin — once a junior on the Thornwall council — into the woods to build a new life off the roads the Vanguard controlled. The settlement is small, perhaps fifty people, and determined not to be found. It is three days' walk from Thornwall by a route only foresters know. Maren has known about it for a year and has said nothing to anyone.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Hollow's Rest feels like a village grown out of the forest rather than built on top of it. The tree-fall hollow is a natural amphitheater: three ancient trunks, each wide as a cottage, collapsed across one another a century ago and rotted down into a broad bowl of deep moss and rich loam, ringed by the standing forest. The settlers have built into this bowl without cutting the living trees — low-roofed cottages of salvaged timber and woven wattle nestled between root-arches, paths of flat stone pressed into moss, a shared cook-hearth at the center in a circle of river stones. The canopy overhead is still mostly intact in this pocket — the crystal corruption has not reached this section of the woods, for reasons no one understands. Light falls in dappled greens. Woodsmoke rises gently through the canopy and disperses before it can be seen from outside. In the son's era, with so much else dying, Hollow's Rest feels strangely alive — not thriving, but persistent, a lung still breathing in a body that is failing.
template_properties.history: The three ancient trunks fell in a single storm about a century ago. Foresters marked the site on old maps as the Great Fall and left it alone; the tangle was impassable and the wood already claimed by rot. Three years before Aric's quest, as the tremors worsened and the Vanguard's checkpoint at Ashenford made movement between the lowlands and the mining settlements effectively impossible, small groups began to slip away from Thornwall and the outlying hamlets, trying to live at the forest's edge. Torbin, then seventy and newly widowed, led one such group. He remembered an old forester's song that described a safe hollow in the deep woods, and — guided by Maren's father's maps in his last year — found the Great Fall. The settlement is two years old. It has one midwife, two skilled hunters, a former blacksmith who shoes horses when anyone has them, and Physician Wren, who came three months ago from a collapsed coastal village and whose skill with herbs has saved more than one life already.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Hearth Circle — a ring of river stones at the center of the hollow with a shared cook-fire. The only fire in Hollow's Rest at night; all smaller hearths go cold after dark to keep the settlement invisible from above.
- Torbin's Cottage — the largest of the dwellings, built into the root-arch of the northern trunk. Contains the settlement's shared records: who is owed what, who is sick, who has gone out and not come back. Also contains a sealed leather tube that Torbin has not opened.
- The Old Midwife's Hearth — a smaller cottage on the east side of the hollow where the settlement's midwife and Physician Wren keep the herb stores and tend the sick.
- The Salvage Lean-To — an open-sided shed where Scavenger Mott stores what he brings back from the roads: Vanguard-dropped supplies, abandoned farm tools, salt, nails. A rough ledger tracks what is owed.
- The Listening Stones — a cluster of three large stones at the hollow's southern edge that catch the wind differently and, the settlers swear, give early warning when something large moves through the forest toward them. Children are sent here to keep watch during the day.
- The Hidden Path — the only access to Hollow's Rest, a route that Maren can lead someone along but that is invisible to anyone else. It winds through swampy low ground, uses a fallen log as a bridge, and crosses a stream at a point where footprints don't hold.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective.
