Deep-forest-settlement tiles: massive fallen trunk tiles (each 4-5 tiles wide, moss-covered, with ferns growing from the rotting bark), root-arch dwelling tiles (cottages built into root systems, with woven-wattle walls and living-root lintels), deep-moss ground tiles in varied greens, river-stone path tiles laid into moss, a shared central hearth tile with a low stone ring and embers visible. Canopy overlay tiles cast dappled green-gold shadow across the ground. Wattle-and-daub cottage walls with small unshuttered windows. No banners, no signs — the settlement is unmarked. Smoke wisps from the central hearth, thin and dispersing into canopy. The edges of the settlement fade into standing forest via transitional tiles (thick undergrowth, fallen logs, low mist). Children sprites, two hunter sprites, the elder at his cottage door. Palette: rich greens, warm browns, muted ambers, low-saturation earth tones — the visual opposite of Ashenford's military gray.
creative_prompts.ambience: A deliberately quieter soundscape than the rest of the son's-era woods. The settlement exists in a pocket of still-healthy forest, so the familiar whispering of the Whispering Woods is present but at normal pitch — not the higher-pitched urgent whisper that marks the corruption zones. Small wood fires crackle at low volume. A child's voice, hushed, carries briefly before a parent shushes it. Someone splitting kindling at slow intervals. A broom on packed earth. The midwife's mortar grinding herbs. A hunter sharpening an arrowhead. Birds still sing here — real ones, not absent like the corruption zones — but softly, as if they too have learned to keep down. No loud voices. No hammering. No ringing bells. The absence of machine-sound is the signature of the place: Hollow's Rest is the sound of people living at the same volume as the forest around them.
creative_prompts.music: Solo mandolin in G dorian mode, played fingerstyle at a walking tempo. The melody is circular and contemplative, built around a simple four-bar phrase that repeats with minor variations — the sound of patience, of people who have decided to stay in one place for as long as they can. A second voice enters in the second pass, a low-tuned whistle or recorder carrying a counter-melody in thirds underneath the mandolin, suggesting quiet companionship. There is no percussion and no drone; the piece drifts in and out of time with its own breathing. Where Thornwall's folk theme in the son's era is lonely and the Vanguard march in Ashenford is oppressive, Hollow's Rest is neither. It is a piece of music that sounds like a deep breath, held, and slowly released.

template_properties.overview: A jagged mountain range north of Thornwall, riddled with abandoned mine tunnels and sealed caverns. The Iron Peaks are the source of the tremors threatening the region and the destination of both Kaelen's and Aric's journeys. In Kaelen's era, the peaks are dangerous but stable — the mines are abandoned, the passes are navigable, and the sealed caverns are curiosities. In Aric's era, the mountains have fractured: new crevasses split the trails, dark energy leaks from the sealed caverns, and creatures not seen in living memory emerge from the deep.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Raw, vertical, and exposed. The lower slopes are forested with twisted iron-bark pines. Above the treeline, bare rock faces are streaked with rust-colored mineral deposits that give the peaks their name. The air thins quickly. In Kaelen's era, the peaks feel abandoned but not hostile — old mine entrances, rusted rail tracks, collapsed headframes. In Aric's era, the same landscape feels actively threatening — the rock has cracked open, faint purple light pulses from deep fissures, and the iron-bark pines near the fissures have petrified into stone.
template_properties.history: The Iron Peaks were mined for iron and copper for centuries by a now-scattered mining guild. The deepest tunnels were sealed two generations ago when miners broke through into a natural cavern system filled with a substance they couldn't identify — a crystalline material that hummed and produced faint light. Three miners died from exposure to the crystal's emissions. The guild sealed the breach, abandoned the mines, and the miners' families founded Thornwall. The sealed caverns have been a local legend ever since. Kaelen's father was one of the last miners to work the upper tunnels before the full abandonment.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Sealed Caverns — the deepest point of the old mines, bricked up two generations ago. Whatever is inside is the source of the tremors. In father's era: sealed, inert. In son's era: the seals are cracking, light and sound leak through.
- The Upper Mines — abandoned mine tunnels used as the first dungeon area. Relatively safe in father's era (bats, cave-ins). In son's era: infested with crystal-mutated creatures.
- The Iron Pass — the main trail over the peaks to the northern regions. In father's era: passable with care. In son's era: partially collapsed, requiring alternate routes.
- Lookout Ridge — a flat outcrop with a view of Thornwall below. Used by both Kaelen and Aric as a milestone marker. In son's era, the view reveals how much the northern farms have deteriorated.
- The Petrified Grove — iron-bark pines near the fissures that have turned to stone. Only exists in Aric's era.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Exterior mountain tiles: gray-brown rock faces with rust-orange mineral streaks, twisted dark-green iron-bark pines on the lower slopes, gravel trails winding upward. Mine entrance tiles: dark wood framing, rusted iron rail tracks, collapsed timber supports. Interior mine tiles: rough stone walls, wooden support beams, puddles reflecting torchlight. Father's era palette: natural grays, browns, and greens with warm torchlight interiors. Son's era additions: purple-tinted fissure tiles that glow faintly, petrified tree tiles (gray stone replacing green canopy), cracked ground tiles with light bleeding through, crystal-growth tiles on cave walls (pale violet, geometric). The contrast between eras should be visible at a glance — natural browns vs. corrupted purples.
creative_prompts.ambience: Exterior: wind howling through rock channels (pitch varies with elevation), distant rock falls, gravel crunching underfoot, eagle cries. Father's era: wind dominates, natural mountain sounds. Son's era: add a low, rhythmic pulse from the sealed caverns (like a distant heartbeat), crystalline chiming from the fissures, and the absence of animal sounds at higher elevations. Interior mines: dripping water echoing, footsteps on stone and gravel, timber creaking under pressure. Son's era mines add: creature sounds (skittering, low growls), crystal resonance hum, and occasional deep rumbles that shake pebbles loose from the ceiling.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: sparse percussion and low brass — taiko-style drums with a French horn playing a slow, ascending melody in B minor. Evokes scale and isolation, the smallness of a person against mountains. Son's era: same drums but with added distortion and uneven timing (suggesting instability), the horn melody replaced by a bowed bass played with heavy vibrato. A high, crystalline synth pad adds an alien quality. The music should feel like the mountain itself is an antagonist.

template_properties.overview: A stone keep perched on a granite outcrop between the northern slopes of the Iron Peaks and the cliffs that hold Starlight Academy. Lastwatch was built three centuries ago as a joint-militia observation post, tasked with watching the high passes for rockfalls, avalanches, and anything that came up out of the deep tunnels that the miners would not talk about. In Kaelen's era, Lastwatch is still maintained — a small garrison of four soldiers and a warden who rotates from Ashenford, the Academy, and two coastal militias. In Aric's era, Lastwatch has been abandoned by every faction except one woman who refuses to leave, and the keep itself is half-consumed by crystal growths that have climbed up out of the rock it was built on. It is the last settlement — if one person counts as a settlement — between the corrupted lowlands and the Academy's cliffs.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Lastwatch is a compact stone keep — a central three-story tower, a walled bailey about forty feet across, a stable block, a cistern, and a signal platform atop the tower with a great iron brazier for warning-fire. The stone is dark gray granite quarried from the outcrop itself. Wind is constant here; the keep sits exposed above the treeline, and the banners have worn to ribbons decades ago. The view from the signal platform is extraordinary: Iron Peaks to the south, the distant pale glow of Starlight Academy on its cliffs to the east, and the Moonlit Sea a gray thread on the farthest horizon. In Kaelen's era, the keep is austere but warm — a garrison of four and a warden, coal fires in every room, the smell of stew and leather polish. In Aric's era, the keep is broken. The outer wall is cracked open on the north face. Crystal growths the size of a man have come up through the foundations and through the floors of the lower rooms. The old stables are a lattice of pale violet. Only the warden's tower room and the signal platform above remain free of the corruption — and only because Jessa Vale has kept them so by means no one has yet explained.
template_properties.history: Lastwatch was commissioned by a joint council of the three lowland militias, the Starlight Academy, and the coastal port guilds (Saltmere included) three centuries ago, after the old mining guild sealed the deep tunnels and abandoned the Iron Peaks. The stated mission was to watch for anything that came up out of the sealed tunnels. For most of its history, nothing did, and Lastwatch settled into a routine of weather-watching and message-relaying — a signal fire from the platform could reach Ashenford, the Academy, and Saltmere within the hour. Wardens rotated through on two-year tours. Fifty years ago, a warden named Hesper Vale saw something at the signal fire and sent the wrong message, and the keep remembers her name in the stones of the courtyard where it is carved. Her granddaughter Jessa took her first posting here thirty years ago and has not left. In the father's era, Jessa Vale has been warden for roughly a decade. In the son's era, she has been warden for thirty years and is the only living soul at the keep.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Signal Platform — atop the central tower, an open stone deck with an iron brazier mounted in a rotating frame so that the warning-fire can be aimed at a specific faction. Pulleys beneath the brazier allow the warden to quick-flash the fire in coded signals. In both eras, this is the only piece of the keep that remains perfectly functional.
- The Warden's Tower Room — one level below the signal platform, Jessa Vale's quarters. A narrow bed, a writing desk with three decades of logs, a cold hearth (she has not lit a fire here in years), and a single window facing the Iron Peaks.
- The Great Hall — the keep's main room on the ground floor, once used for joint-militia meetings. In father's era: a long timber table, four iron-banded chairs, a wall map of the passes. In son's era: crystal growths have come up through the floor and taken the table down one leg at a time; the map is still on the wall, fossilized in a thin layer of pale violet.
- The Armory — a stone-vaulted chamber built into the keep's north wall, where Holt (the keep armorer) maintains the garrison's weapons in the father's era. In son's era: the armory is sealed behind a crystal wall of growth, and its contents are inaccessible without breaking through.
- The Cistern — a deep rainwater cistern beneath the bailey, fed by runoff from the tower roof. In father's era: a steady water source. In son's era: the water has gone silver-gray with sediment and is undrinkable; Jessa fetches her water from a spring a quarter-mile south.
- Hesper's Stone — a slab in the courtyard carved with the name of Jessa's grandmother. Jessa visits it daily in both eras. In the son's era, the stone is ringed by a small circle that the crystal growths have not crossed — the only other place in Lastwatch, besides the tower and platform, that remains clear.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Keep exterior tiles: dark gray granite block walls with narrow arrow slits, a central tower (roughly 6x6 tiles at base, rising three stories in a stacked vertical layout), outer bailey walls (3 tiles tall for a top-down implied-height effect), stable block with slate roof tiles, cistern cap tile (a round iron grate in the courtyard). Signal platform atop the tower: a flat stone deck with a mounted iron brazier prop, rope-and-pulley rigging visible. Father's era palette: cold granite gray, slate dark-blue roof, warm oil-lamp light from tower windows, red-and-gold warning-fire brazier, snow patches in the shaded lee of the walls depending on season. Son's era additions: cracked outer wall tiles on the north face with rubble spilling inward, crystal-growth tiles (pale violet, geometric, roughly 1-3 tiles per cluster) pushing up through flagstones, stables entirely overtaken by crystal lattice, a sealed-over armory door tile where the crystal has grown across it. The warden's tower room and signal platform remain free of crystal — their tiles are unchanged from the father's era, a small island of preserved stone in a keep that has been otherwise claimed. Hesper's Stone in the courtyard has a visible clear-ring around it in son's era tilework.
creative_prompts.ambience: Exterior: high-altitude wind as the dominant layer, varying in pitch with wall geometry — moaning through arrow slits, whistling past the signal platform brazier-frame, dying in the lee of the tower and rising again at the corners. Distant rockfall in the Iron Peaks to the south, more frequent in son's era. Faint, very distant gull cries from the coast, audible only when the wind direction is right. Father's era interior: the ordinary sounds of a tiny garrison — four pairs of boots on stone floors, a soldier sharpening a blade, Holt's hammer from the armory, a cook stirring a pot, wood crackling in the great hall's hearth, Jessa's voice occasionally giving instructions. Son's era interior: silence, broken only by Jessa's footsteps (slow now) and her writing quill on the log book. A new constant: the crystal growths at the keep's core emit a faint, continuous high-frequency hum — at the edge of perception when one first arrives, increasingly noticeable as one stays longer. It is not unpleasant. It is also not leaving.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: a slow, formal piece in C minor for solo French horn and low strings. The horn carries a melody that rises in wide intervals, pauses, and descends — the musical equivalent of a watchman's scan across a horizon. Low strings underneath provide a sustained drone rather than chord changes; the harmony moves slowly, patiently. A distant bell-like note (glockenspiel or tubular chime) enters at the end of each phrase, marking time. The piece feels vigilant rather than dramatic — the music of something that has been listening for three hundred years and expects to listen for three hundred more. Son's era: the same horn melody, but played with a thinner tone and longer pauses between phrases. The low strings are gone. The bell-like punctuation is replaced by the high-frequency hum of the crystal growths, which sits just above the horn's range and never resolves. In the second half of the loop, a new instrument enters — a single female voice, wordless, singing a counter-melody that is clearly related to the horn but not the same. This is Jessa Vale's presence in the music. She does not sing in the fiction. The voice is the keep remembering her.

template_properties.overview: A working fishing and trading port on the eastern coast, half a day's travel southeast of Thornwall along the old coast road. Saltmere is built where a chalk river empties into the Moonlit Sea, its two stone breakwaters curving out to shelter a deep-water harbor. In Kaelen's era it is the busiest inland-coast port in the region — fishing crews come in on the morning tide, coastal traders unload cloth and salt, and the harbormaster's office logs a ship a day. In Aric's era the harbor is silting up: mineral sediment washes down from the corrupted uplands and settles in the shallows, the catch has thinned, and half the docks have rotted. What remains operates on private arrangements rather than guild charter.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Saltmere smells of fish, tar, and wet rope. Timber warehouses stand shoulder-to-shoulder along the waterfront, their sides weathered silver-gray from salt spray. Above the warehouses rise the older stone buildings of the merchant quarter — three-story townhouses with iron balconies overlooking the harbor. The streets are paved with worn flagstones, uneven where the salt has lifted the mortar. In Kaelen's era, fishing boats and coastal traders crowd the harbor, ropes whipping in the sea wind, and the harbormaster's tower flies the port's blue-and-white pennant. In Aric's era, the same harbor holds perhaps a third as many vessels, many of them half-sunk or stripped for parts. A pale film coats the shallows where the river meets the sea, and the water's edge is crusted with a thin scum of mineral residue that crunches underfoot like frost.
template_properties.history: Saltmere began as a seasonal fishing camp two centuries ago, before the Thornbriar settlements existed. Its permanent population grew when inland traders started routing their coastal shipments through the chalk-river mouth rather than the more dangerous cliffs further north. The port's guild charter was signed eighty years ago, establishing Saltmere as a self-governing trade town under the protection — but not the jurisdiction — of the lowland militias. The Vanguard established a quartering right here a generation ago, never much enforced, but in the son's era the Vanguard has begun pressing that right to control what moves through the harbor. Three years before Aric's quest begins, the chalk river started running cloudy and the shallower fishing grounds collapsed. The guild has not officially acknowledged this yet. Everyone knows.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Twin Breakwaters — two curving stone moles extending a quarter-mile into the Moonlit Sea, lined with mooring bollards. In father's era: a beacon fire burns on the seaward end of each mole. In son's era: only the landward beacon is still lit.
- The Harbormaster's Tower — a four-story stone tower at the base of the north breakwater, flying the port pennant. Cael Vire's office is on the third floor. Contains two decades of harbor logs that Aric can consult.
- The Low Docks — the older, wooden docks on the southern side of the harbor, used mainly by fishing crews. Cheaper mooring, less supervision. In son's era: half rotted out, the remaining slips used by smaller traders who prefer not to be logged.
- The Saltscale Inn — the port's main inn, owned by Brine, on the waterfront between the Low Docks and the merchant quarter. Large common room, rooms above the kitchen, a private back table reserved for harbormaster business.
- The Chalk Mouth — where the river meets the sea. A wide, shallow estuary with reed beds and a derelict tide mill. In father's era: the tide mill is operational and grinds salt. In son's era: the mill is abandoned and the estuary water runs silver-gray with suspended sediment.
- Nella's Fish Stall — a permanent wooden stall at the foot of the Low Docks, where Nella has sold dockside fish for thirty years. Unofficial gossip center of the town.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Coastal town tiles: gray-silver weathered timber warehouse walls with rust-stained metal braces, worn flagstone streets with patches of wet stone, sloping wooden dock tiles with rope bollard props, stone breakwater tiles curving at the edges. Harbor water tiles: a clear teal-blue for deep water in the father's era, graduated to a muddy gray-green in the shallows, with foam-edge tiles at the dock lines. Fishing boat sprites: small single-masted dinghies, larger two-masted coastal traders, nets draped over gunwales. Father's era palette: saturated sea blues, warm tan wood, white-painted mooring posts, colorful pennants (red, yellow, blue). Son's era additions: silted shallow-water tiles with a pale mineral film, rotted dock tiles with gap-toothed planks, abandoned boat hulls leaning against pilings, scum-crusted waterline tiles. The chalk river estuary gets special tiles: in father's era clear turquoise water with green reed beds, in son's era a silver-gray silted outflow with dead reed stubble. Harbormaster's tower is a tall stone landmark visible from anywhere in the port.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: a layered working-port soundscape. Gulls calling overhead in overlapping choruses, waves slapping against pilings at slow intervals, rope creaking against bollards, ship bells at varied pitches and distances, the rhythmic thunk of a caulker's hammer, a fishmonger calling prices, rope reels clacking, hulls bumping dock rubrails. Undergirding it all is the constant low rush of surf on the breakwaters. Son's era: the same base soundscape but thinned and distorted. Fewer gulls, spaced farther apart. The caulker's hammer is absent. The fishmonger's call is heard only once, not repeated. The breakwater surf has a new quality — a faint hiss where the sediment in the water froths differently against the stones. Add a new low sound: the slow grinding shift of silted mud in the shallows, audible only when the tide pulls back.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: a sea-shanty-inflected piece in D major, led by a concertina (accordion family) playing a lilting melody at a moderate walking tempo. Hand drums and a tambourine provide a rolling, oceanic rhythm. A tin whistle adds ornamental trills over the main melody like birds over the harbor. The piece has a verse-chorus feel — expect villagers to hum it. Son's era: the same concertina melody, but slower, fewer ornaments, and the hand drum is replaced by a single bass drum struck softly at long intervals like a distant bell buoy. The tin whistle is gone. A solo fiddle enters in the second half of the loop with a counter-melody in the relative minor, adding a note of stubborn endurance — the port has not given up, even if the sea has changed.

template_properties.overview: An ancient academy perched on a cliff overlooking the Moonlit Sea, founded by the Starweavers — scholars who could read meaning in the patterns of starlight. The Academy is a sprawling complex of white stone towers, covered walkways, and underground archives. It serves as both a center of learning and a repository for dangerous knowledge. The west wing has been sealed for thirty years since Elder Aldric's failed ritual. The Academy appears in the father's era as a place Kaelen visits for information and in the son's era as a diminished institution struggling to remain relevant.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: The Academy is built from pale limestone that glows faintly blue under starlight — a natural property of the cliff stone, not magic. During the day, it looks like weathered white marble. At night, the entire complex takes on a soft luminescence. The architecture is vertical: narrow towers with spiral staircases, observation platforms at every level, and glass-domed reading rooms. The interior is filled with star charts, orreries, and astronomical instruments. The Sealed West Wing is visible from the courtyard — its windows are dark, its doors are chained, and ivy has claimed its lower walls.
template_properties.history: Founded five hundred years ago by the Starweavers, a group of scholars who discovered that certain starlight patterns corresponded to events in the physical world. They built the Academy to study these patterns and developed a system of celestial cartography. Over centuries, the Academy's influence waned as the Starweaver bloodline thinned. Aldric's failed ritual thirty years ago was an attempt to access the Sealed Observatory at the top of the west wing, believed to contain the founders' master charts. The ritual's failure killed three scholars and collapsed the connecting bridge, effectively sealing the entire wing.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Great Library — the largest collection of star charts in the known world, spanning three underground levels. Accessible in both eras.
- The Observatory (East) — a functioning observation dome where students study celestial patterns. Aldric teaches here.
- The Sealed West Wing — chained doors, dark windows, ivy-covered walls. Contains the Sealed Observatory at its peak. In son's era: the chains have rusted, and faint light occasionally flickers in the upper windows.
- The Courtyard of Charts — an open-air space with constellations inlaid in the stone floor. Used for outdoor lectures and ceremonies.
- The Archive Vaults — deep underground chambers containing texts deemed too dangerous for the main library. Only Aldric has the key.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Exterior: pale white-blue stone tiles, narrow tower structures with pointed roofs, covered walkway tiles connecting buildings, cliff-edge tiles with sea visible below. Interior library: dark wood bookshelves, reading desks with candles, star-chart scrolls on walls, orrery props (brass spheres on mechanical arms). Interior sealed wing: darker palette — dusty floors, cobwebbed furniture, cracked walls, chains across doorways. The courtyard uses special floor tiles with constellation patterns inlaid in gold against white stone. Night variant: all exterior tiles gain a soft blue luminescence, windows glow warm yellow. The contrast between the lived-in east wing (warm, lit, populated) and the sealed west wing (cold, dark, abandoned) should be stark even in top-down view.
creative_prompts.ambience: Exterior: sea wind, distant waves crashing on the cliff base, seabirds, the mechanical clicking of an orrery from an open window, faint sound of turning pages. Interior library: hushed — soft footsteps on stone, pages turning, quill on parchment, occasional whispered conversation, the low hum of astronomical instruments. Sealed wing: silence, then: wind through broken glass, stone settling, a faint resonance from above (the Sealed Observatory), rats. The sealed wing should feel like held breath — something waiting.
creative_prompts.music: Ethereal strings and soft choir in D major, slow tempo. A solo violin plays the main theme — elegant, ascending, suggesting aspiration and accumulated knowledge. Glass harmonica provides the characteristic Academy shimmer. The arrangement thins to solo piano when near the sealed wing, transposing to D minor. The choir disappears, the strings become a single sustained drone. Near the Sealed Observatory: the music drops out entirely, replaced by a single, pure tone — the resonance of whatever is sealed inside.

template_properties.overview: A fortified farming village at the base of the Iron Peaks, named for the centuries-old thornbriar hedge that forms its outer wall. Thornwall is the starting location for both the father and son's stories. In Kaelen's era, it is a modest but thriving settlement of about two hundred people — a militia outpost, a market square, and farmland stretching south. In Aric's era twenty years later, Thornwall has contracted: the northern farms are abandoned, the thornbriar wall is patchy and dying, and the population has halved as families fled the worsening tremors.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: In the father's era, Thornwall feels safe and lived-in. The thornbriar hedge is dense and green, cottages have warm hearth-light, children play near the well, and the militia practices sword forms in the square. The Iron Peaks loom to the north, snow-capped and distant. In the son's era, the same village feels hollowed out. The hedge has gaps where the briars have rotted. Abandoned cottages are boarded up. The well water tastes of iron. The militia barracks is half-empty. The Iron Peaks seem closer — their silhouette darker, as if the mountains have leaned in.
template_properties.history: Thornwall was founded three generations ago by miners who abandoned the Iron Peaks after the deep tunnels became unstable. They settled at the mountain's base and turned to farming, using the natural thornbriar growth as a defensive perimeter. The village grew slowly, serving as a waypoint between the coastal trade routes and the mountain passes. Kaelen's militia heroics put Thornwall on the map; traders started making detours to visit "the hero's village." After Kaelen left, the village's fame faded, and the returning tremors drove away those who had come for the legend.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Thornbriar Wall — a living hedge of ancient thornbriar, 10 feet tall and 4 feet thick, encircling the village. In father's era: dense, flowering. In son's era: patchy, with dead sections.
- The Market Square — center of village life. In father's era: busy with traders. In son's era: mostly empty, used for militia drills.
- The Militia Barracks — where Kaelen trained. Contains his old journal (found by Aric at age twelve). In son's era: half the bunks are empty.
- The Elder's Cottage — home of the village elder who raised Aric. Contains maps and records of Thornwall's history.
- The Northern Farms — fertile land stretching toward the Iron Peaks. In father's era: golden wheat fields. In son's era: fallow, cracked earth, and sinkholes.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Father's era: warm earth tones (golden wheat, green thornbriar, warm brown cottages with orange hearth-glow in windows). Cobblestone paths between buildings, a central well with a stone rim. Market stalls with colored awnings. Children sprites near the well. Militia guards at the gate. The thornbriar wall is a thick, flowering green border. Mountains visible in the background tile, snow-capped and distant. Son's era: same layout but desaturated — wheat fields replaced with cracked brown earth, thornbriar wall has gray dead sections, boarded-up cottages with dark windows, fewer NPC sprites, the well has a rusty bucket. Mountains in background are darker, closer-feeling.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: birdsong, distant hammer on anvil, children laughing, wind through wheat, occasional cow lowing, merchant calling prices. A warm, alive soundscape. Son's era: wind through gaps in the thornbriar, a single crow cawing, distant rumble from the mountains (subtle, irregular), creaking of boarded windows, dripping water. The same base sounds but thinned out — fewer layers, more silence between events. Both eras share the sound of the well's bucket chain.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: folk ensemble in D major — acoustic guitar, pennywhistle, and hand drum. Upbeat tempo, communal feel, like a village gathering song. Melodic and hummable. Son's era: same melody transposed to D minor, played on solo acoustic guitar with no accompaniment. Slower tempo, more space between notes. The familiar tune made lonely. A faint, low string drone appears in the second half, suggesting the distant tremors beneath the earth.

template_properties.overview: A dense old-growth forest stretching between Thornwall and the coastal regions. The woods are named for the constant rustling of their canopy, which locals say sounds like murmured conversation. In Kaelen's era, the Whispering Woods are healthy and navigable — a source of timber, game, and medicinal herbs. Maren's family has patrolled these woods for generations. In Aric's era, the forest is dying from the inside out. Trees rot while standing, animal populations have collapsed, and sinkholes open without warning. The whispering has changed — it now sounds less like conversation and more like warning.
template_properties.atmosphere_and_appearance: Multi-layered canopy forest with massive trunks and filtered green light. The forest floor is soft with moss and fallen leaves. Streams thread between root systems. In the father's era: lush, vibrant greens, dappled golden sunlight, wildflowers along deer trails, bird nests visible in lower branches. In the son's era: the canopy has thinned, letting in harsh white light. Bark peels from trunks revealing gray wood underneath. Streams run slower and cloudier. The moss has patches of black fungal growth. Sinkholes are visible as dark circles in the forest floor, some ringed with crystalline growth similar to the Iron Peaks fissures.
template_properties.history: The Whispering Woods predate Thornwall by centuries. Local lore holds that the trees were planted by a civilization that existed before the current age — the roots go impossibly deep, and the oldest trunks are wider than cottages. The foresters who tend the woods have found carved stones deep in the undergrowth, suggesting ancient structures now buried beneath root systems. Maren's father discovered that the sinkholes appearing in the son's era follow the pattern of these buried structures, as if something below is pushing upward through the ancient foundations.
template_properties.notable_features:
- The Canopy Trail — an elevated path through the treetops, maintained by foresters. In father's era: a safe travel route. In son's era: sections have collapsed where supporting trees died.
- The Moss Clearing — a natural amphitheater where the forest floor dips and moss grows thick. Used by foresters as a campsite. In son's era: a sinkhole has opened at its center.
- The Ancient Stones — carved stone markers half-buried in roots, inscribed with symbols no one can read. Found throughout the deep woods.
- The Forester's Cabin — Maren's family home, at the forest's edge near Thornwall. In father's era: well-maintained. In son's era: Maren lives here alone, maintaining it stubbornly.
- The Deep Root — the oldest tree in the forest, its trunk thirty feet across. Local legend says its roots reach underground water. In son's era: still alive but visibly stressed, its bark cracked and weeping sap.

creative_prompts.environment-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective. Dense forest tiles: massive brown tree trunks (some 3-4 tiles wide for the ancient trees), green canopy overlay that partially obscures the ground layer, moss-covered ground tiles in varied greens, stream tiles (clear blue in father's era, gray-brown in son's era), wildflower accent tiles (father's era only). Path tiles: dirt trails through undergrowth, elevated wooden walkway tiles for the canopy trail. Son's era additions: dead tree tiles (gray bark, bare branches), sinkhole tiles (dark circular holes with crumbled edges), black fungus overlay on moss tiles, crystal growth tiles near sinkholes (same pale violet as Iron Peaks). The forest should feel dense — limited visibility, lots of layered tiles creating depth. Father's era palette: rich greens, warm browns, golden light. Son's era palette: muted greens, gray-browns, cool white light.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: a rich, layered forest soundscape. Wind through leaves creating the signature "whispering" — a constant, organic murmur. Bird calls (varied species at different distances), stream babbling, insect buzz, wood creaking, occasional animal movement in undergrowth. Footsteps on soft ground. Son's era: the whispering is still present but higher-pitched, more urgent. Fewer bird calls — long silences between them. The stream sound is thinner. Add: wood cracking and groaning (dying trees), a low subsonic rumble near sinkholes, crystal resonance chiming near fungal growth patches. The absence of the full animal chorus should feel wrong — a forest that has lost its voice.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: layered woodwinds — two recorders playing in thirds over a finger-picked lute in E minor. The arrangement is gentle and cyclical, like walking in circles through familiar paths. Natural forest sounds bleed into the music — the boundary between soundtrack and ambience is deliberately blurred. Son's era: solo recorder playing the same melody, but with longer pauses and slower tempo. The lute is replaced by a low, bowed psaltery that adds tension. The melody occasionally breaks into dissonance — a wrong note, quickly corrected — suggesting the forest's corruption bleeding into the music itself.

template_properties.overview: The mountain trail ascending from the Whispering Woods tree line to the abandoned mine entrances of the Iron Peaks. A steep, winding path carved into the rock face with switchbacks and exposed ridgelines. Lookout Ridge — a flat outcrop with a panoramic view of Thornwall below — sits midway up. This zone transitions from forest to bare mountain, with increasing danger and decreasing vegetation as the player climbs.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Tall zone (20x24 tiles), oriented with the trail climbing from south to north. Southern quarter: tree line transition — scattered iron-bark pines on rocky ground. Middle: bare rock face with a switchback trail carved into it (2-tile wide path zigzagging up). Lookout Ridge is a flat 4x4 platform on the eastern edge at the midpoint. Northern quarter: the trail reaches a plateau with the mine entrance (a dark opening in the rock face, 2 tiles wide). Impassable: cliff edges (drop-offs), large boulders, sheer rock faces. The trail itself is narrow — limited room for combat, environmental hazards (loose rocks, narrow ledges).
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- Mountain goats (passive, ambient)
- 1-2 hostile encounters (mountain bandits or rock creatures)
- Lookout Ridge: rest point, vista dialogue (Kaelen comments on Thornwall below)
- Mine entrance: locked/blocked, requires story progression to open

**Son's era:**
- Crystal-mutated creatures (tougher than forest encounters)
- Lookout Ridge: Aric comments on Thornwall's deterioration (the northern farms are visible as brown patches)
- Fissures in the rock face (new — glowing purple cracks, impassable but story-relevant)
- Mine entrance: partially collapsed but passable
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Iron-bark pines at tree line: green, alive → some petrified (stone-gray)
- Rock face: natural gray-brown → cracked with purple fissure lines
- Trail: intact with carved steps → some sections crumbled, alternate paths needed
- Lookout Ridge: clean stone platform → cracked, a fissure runs through the eastern edge
- Mine entrance: solid wooden door frame → collapsed timbers, dark opening
- Sky/background: clear mountain sky → slightly hazy, darker

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: rocky ground (gray-brown with rust-orange mineral streaks), carved trail tiles (smoother stone, lighter than surrounding rock, with step-edge details), cliff edge tiles (sharp drop-off shown as dark shadow gradient), iron-bark pine tiles (twisted dark trunk, compact green canopy — smaller than Whispering Woods trees), boulder tiles (large round rock, 1x1 and 2x2 variants), switchback turn tiles (trail bends, carved stone railing on outer edge), Lookout Ridge platform tiles (flat polished stone, lighter color, carved railing), mine entrance tiles (dark opening framed by timber supports, rusted iron rail tracks emerging). Son's era additions: petrified pine tiles (same shape but stone-gray with no green), fissure tiles (purple-violet line cracks in rock, emitting faint glow), crumbled trail tiles (broken edge, rubble), collapsed mine frame (fallen timbers, partially blocked opening). The zone should convey verticality — lighter tiles at top (sky exposure), darker at bottom (shadow). The trail should feel like a thin line of safety through hostile terrain.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: wind dominance — constant, gusting, changing pitch with elevation. Rocky footsteps (hard, echoing against cliff faces). Gravel sliding underfoot on switchbacks. Eagle cry (distant, above). Iron-bark pine needles rustling (sparse, not like the forest's thick canopy). Occasional rock fall (small stones bouncing down). At Lookout Ridge: wind subsides slightly, distant Thornwall sounds barely audible (a hint of the village theme). Son's era: wind is stronger, with a whistling quality through the fissures. Crystal resonance from the fissures (a sustained, eerie tone that varies with wind). Rock fall sounds are more frequent and larger. Petrified pines make no sound (they're stone — the absence of rustling is noticeable). Creature sounds from the mine entrance area. At Lookout Ridge: a low rumble from within the mountain, felt more than heard.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: sparse percussion (taiko-style) and solo French horn in B minor. Slow ascending melody matching the climb. The horn sounds isolated against wind — one person against the mountain. Percussion hits on switchback turns (rhythmic accent for the climb). At Lookout Ridge: the melody opens into a brief major key phrase — a moment of beauty before the final push. Loop: 120 seconds. Son's era: same taiko but with unstable timing (hits slightly off-beat, suggesting instability). Horn replaced by bowed bass with heavy vibrato. Crystal synth pad rises with elevation. The melody descends where father's ascended — the mountain is pushing back. At Lookout Ridge: the music pauses, replaced by the mountain's rumble. Loop: 120 seconds.

template_properties.overview: The first dungeon floor — abandoned mine tunnels carved into the Iron Peaks generations ago. Narrow passages, support timbers, rusted rail tracks, and collapsed sections create a maze-like environment. The player navigates through to reach the deeper levels and eventually the Sealed Cavern. In the father's era, the mines are abandoned but structurally sound. In the son's era, crystal growth has invaded the tunnels, creatures have nested, and some passages have collapsed while new ones have opened.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Medium-large zone (24x20 tiles). Network of interconnected tunnels radiating from a central hub (the old mineshaft elevator room, 4x4 tiles, non-functional). Four main tunnel branches: north (to Sealed Cavern entrance — blocked in father's era), east (dead end with loot), south (entrance from the Trail), west (collapsed in father's era, opened by crystal growth in son's era — alternate path). Tunnels are 2-3 tiles wide. Rail tracks run along the main passages. Support timbers at regular intervals. The hub has a higher ceiling (visually lighter). Dead-end alcoves contain old mining equipment and potential loot. Walkable: tunnel floors, rail tracks. Impassable: rock walls, collapsed rubble, intact support pillars.
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- Bats (passive, ambient — scatter when approached)
- 1-2 cave spider encounters (basic dungeon enemies)
- Mining equipment interactions (pickaxe — key item, lantern — light source)
- Lore note on wall (miner's warning about the deep tunnels)

**Son's era:**
- Crystal-mutated creatures (cave spiders evolved, more aggressive)
- Crystal growth nodes (interactable — can be broken for resources or examined with Starweaver Lens)
- 1 mini-boss encounter in the west branch (new area opened by crystal growth)
- Environmental hazard: unstable floor sections (shake visual, damage if lingered)
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Tunnel walls: rough stone → stone with crystal veins (violet lines in rock tiles)
- Rail tracks: rusted but intact → some sections buckled by crystal growth pushing from below
- Support timbers: weathered wood → some cracked/broken, crystal growing through gaps
- Hub room: empty, dusty → crystal growth cluster at center (large, ambient light source)
- North passage (to Sealed Cavern): solid brick wall → bricks cracked, violet light leaking through
- West passage: fully collapsed rubble → rubble cleared by crystal growth, new passage opened
- Air: dusty, stale → faint crystal haze, particles visible in light
- Temperature: cool → warm (the crystal generates heat)

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: mine tunnel floor (dark gray stone with gravel texture), mine wall tiles (rough-hewn rock, darker than floor), support timber tiles (brown vertical beams at tunnel edges), rail track tiles (two parallel rust-brown lines on floor), collapsed rubble tiles (mixed rock and timber, impassable), hub room floor (smoother stone, lighter — the worked center of the mine), mining equipment tiles (pickaxe against wall, lantern on hook, barrel, crate), minecart tile (on rails, can be pushed?), elevator shaft tile (dark square with wooden frame, non-functional). Son's era additions: crystal vein wall tiles (stone with glowing violet lines — the crystal is growing through the rock), crystal cluster tiles (larger formations, 1x1 and 2x2, emit pale violet light), crystal-cracked timber tiles (wood split by crystal growth), buckled rail tiles (bent upward by force from below), unstable floor tiles (subtle crack pattern, slightly different color — visual warning), crystal haze overlay (semi-transparent violet tint on affected areas). Lighting: father's era uses warm orange (torch/lantern), son's era mixes orange torchlight with violet crystal glow — the two light sources competing.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: underground echo chamber acoustics. Dripping water (irregular, echoing), footsteps on stone (sharp reverb), bat wing flutters (sudden, startling), wind draft through tunnels (low moan), timber creaking under rock pressure, distant rock settling, minecart wheels on rails (if pushed), lantern flame hiss. The mine should sound hollow and abandoned — every sound echoes. Son's era: same base acoustics but warmer (crystal heat changes the air). Crystal resonance hum (constant, varies by location — louder near clusters), creature skittering (fast, in walls), unstable floor rumble (warning sound before damage), crystal breaking (sharp, glass-like when nodes are destroyed), a deep pulse from below (the Sealed Cavern, rhythmic, like a heartbeat). The crystal hum should become the new normal — silence is when it stops, and that's scarier.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: minimal — mostly ambient with occasional musical stings. A low, sustained cello note as base drone. Sparse pizzicato strings when exploring (plucked notes, irregular rhythm, suggesting careful steps in the dark). Combat encounters: the drone intensifies, percussion enters. The music should feel like the mine is breathing — slow, patient, not hostile but not safe. Loop: 180 seconds with long quiet sections. Son's era: the cello drone is replaced by a synthesized crystal tone (same pitch, different timbre — organic replaced by mineral). Pizzicato strings are joined by crystal percussion (tuned chimes struck with mallets). The rhythm is slightly faster — the mine is more active, more alive. Near the Sealed Cavern entrance: all other music fades, replaced by a deep, throbbing bass note and the crystal hum at its loudest. Loop: 180 seconds.

template_properties.overview: The quieter eastern district of Thornwall, home to the village elder's cottage, a small chapel, and a handful of residential houses. This is where Aric grew up. The Elder Quarter is where key story conversations happen — the elder's cottage contains maps, records, and Kaelen's hidden journal. In the father's era, it's a peaceful residential area. In the son's era, several houses are abandoned, but the elder's cottage remains the beating heart of the community.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Medium zone (16x14 tiles). Flagstone paths connect the buildings. The elder's cottage is the largest structure (4x3 tiles) at the north end, with a small garden patch beside it. A chapel (3x2 tiles) sits at the south end. Four residential cottages (2x2 each) line the east and west sides. A large oak tree (2x2, impassable) grows in the center courtyard. The south exit leads to the Market. No other exits — this is a dead-end district, reflecting its quiet nature.
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- Village elder (in cottage — dialogue: local history, advice for Kaelen)
- 2 residents (ambient, short dialogue)
- Chapel keeper (dialogue: lore about the region's founding)

**Son's era:**
- Village elder (aged, in cottage — dialogue: stories about Kaelen, concern for Aric)
- Aric (found here at certain story points)
- 1 remaining resident
- Kaelen's Journal discovery event (triggered at militia barracks in Market, but the journal content references this area)
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Elder's cottage: well-maintained, garden flourishing → maintained but aged, garden smaller
- Residential cottages: 4 occupied → 2 occupied, 2 boarded up
- Chapel: active, candles lit → still open but dusty, fewer candles
- Oak tree: full green canopy → still alive but leaves are thinning, some bare branches
- Flagstone: clean → moss-covered, some cracked
- Overall: warm, lived-in → quiet, holding on

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: flagstone path (gray-blue, more formal than cobblestone), elder's cottage tiles (larger stone building, thatched roof visible from top-down as brown texture, warm window glow), residential cottage tiles (smaller wood-and-stone, varied roof colors), chapel tiles (pale stone with small steeple shadow), garden tiles (brown earth with green plant rows), oak tree tiles (large trunk center with canopy overlay spreading 2x2), boarded cottage variant (dark windows, boards across door). Flagstone should feel quieter and more dignified than the Market's cobblestone. The elder's cottage should clearly be the most important building — slightly larger, warmer lighting, more detail in the roof tiles.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: flagstone footsteps (softer than cobblestone), wind through the oak tree's leaves, a garden tool clinking, chapel bell (single tone, once per loop), distant laughter from the Market, a door opening and closing, fire crackling from the elder's cottage chimney. Gentle, residential, safe. Son's era: flagstone footsteps, wind through the oak (thinner sound, fewer leaves), elder's chimney fire (still there — the one constant), creaking from abandoned cottages, chapel bell (same tone but sounds lonelier in the quiet), no Market sounds. The elder's chimney fire sound is the emotional anchor — the one thing that hasn't changed.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: gentle harp arpeggios in C major with a solo oboe melody — warm, domestic, unhurried. The oboe suggests wisdom and age (the elder's theme hint). Volume is low — this is background music for conversation scenes. Son's era: same harp pattern but slower, in C minor. The oboe is replaced by a solo clarinet playing the melody an octave lower — the same wisdom made heavier by twenty years. A faint music-box quality on the last phrase suggests childhood memories (Aric's connection to this place).

template_properties.overview: The central square of Thornwall — a cobblestone plaza surrounded by shops, the village well, and the militia notice board. This is the player's hub zone in both eras. In the father's era it bustles with traders, children, and militia guards. In the son's era the same square feels half-empty: stalls are boarded up, the well bucket is rusty, and the notice board is covered in warnings about creature sightings.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: A roughly rectangular plaza (20x16 tiles). The well sits at the center (2x2 impassable). Cobblestone paths radiate from the well to four exits: north (to Thornwall North Gate), south (to the southern road), east (to the Elder Quarter), west (to the farmlands). Market stalls line the north and east edges — in father's era they have awnings and goods; in son's era half are boarded up. The militia barracks is a large building (4x3 tiles) on the west side. A small herb shop sits on the east side near the Elder Quarter exit. The ground is mostly walkable cobblestone with building tiles as impassable walls.
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- 2-3 trader NPCs at market stalls (dialogue: local rumors, item shop)
- 1 militia guard at the north exit (dialogue: warns about mountain paths)
- 2 children playing near the well (ambient, non-interactive)
- Kaelen's starting position: near the militia barracks

**Son's era:**
- 1 remaining trader (limited inventory, pessimistic dialogue)
- 1 elderly militia guard (dialogue: stories about Kaelen, warnings about tremors)
- Maren (companion, found near the herb shop)
- Aric's starting position: near the well
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Market stalls: 6 active → 2 active, 4 boarded (tile swap)
- Well: clean stone rim → rusty bucket, cracked rim (tile swap)
- Militia barracks: full bunks, lit windows → half-empty, dim windows
- NPC count: ~6 → ~3
- Ground: clean cobblestone → some cracked tiles, weeds growing through gaps
- Notice board: trade postings → creature warning posters
- Ambient: lively crowd sounds → wind, occasional footstep

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles needed: cobblestone ground (2-3 variants for visual variety), cobblestone-cracked variant (son's era), building walls (brown wood with stone base), building interiors visible through doorways (dark with warm light), market stall tiles (wooden frame with colored awning — red, blue, green), boarded-up stall variant (gray wood, no awning), well tiles (circular stone rim, dark water center), militia barracks facade (larger stone building, iron-banded door), herb shop facade (wooden with hanging dried plants), notice board (wooden frame with paper rectangles). Era-specific palette: father's era warm (golden cobblestone, warm brown wood, colored awnings), son's era cool (gray-tinted cobblestone, weathered wood, muted colors). Transition tiles needed for exits: cobblestone-to-dirt (south, west), cobblestone-to-flagstone (east to Elder Quarter), cobblestone-to-gate (north).
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: cobblestone footsteps, crowd murmur (3-5 voices at medium distance), merchant calling prices, children laughing near well, metal clinking from militia training, cart wheels on stone, birdsong. A warm, populated soundscape at moderate volume. Son's era: cobblestone footsteps (slower), wind through empty stalls, a single merchant's half-hearted call, creaking wood from boarded stalls, well bucket chain rattling, distant crow caw, occasional low rumble from the mountains. Same spatial layout as father's era but with 60% of the sound layers removed — the silence is the atmosphere.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: folk melody in G major — acoustic guitar and pennywhistle at moderate tempo. Bright, communal, the sound of a village at peace. Melodic hook is simple and memorable — players will hear this often. Loop length: 90 seconds. Son's era: same melody in G minor, solo acoustic guitar, half tempo. The pennywhistle is absent. A faint low drone on the last 4 bars suggests the distant tremors. Loop length: 90 seconds. The recognition of the same tune made sad is the emotional point.

template_properties.overview: The fortified northern entrance to Thornwall, where the thornbriar wall is thickest and a wooden gate controls passage to the Whispering Woods and the road toward the Iron Peaks. A militia guardhouse flanks the gate. This zone serves as a transition between the safe town and the dangerous wilderness. In the father's era, the gate is well-maintained and guarded. In the son's era, the thornbriar has gaps, the gate hangs crooked, and the guardhouse is understaffed.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Narrow zone (16x12 tiles) oriented north-south. The thornbriar wall runs east-west across the middle of the zone, with the gate opening at center (2 tiles wide). The guardhouse is a small building (3x2) on the west side of the gate. A dirt path runs north-south through the gate — south leads to the Market, north leads out to the Whispering Woods Edge. The thornbriar wall tiles are impassable except at the gate. East and west edges are dense thornbriar (impassable). A small weapons rack and training dummy sit outside the guardhouse (interactable in father's era).
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- 2 militia guards (one at gate, one patrolling)
- Training dummy (interactable — tutorial combat hint)
- Weapons rack (interactable — basic equipment)

**Son's era:**
- 1 militia guard (tired, warns about forest creatures)
- Collapsed training dummy (non-interactive flavor)
- Empty weapons rack
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Thornbriar wall: dense, green, flowering → patchy, dead sections (gray tiles), gaps visible
- Gate: solid wood, oiled hinges → warped wood, hanging slightly open
- Guardhouse: lit windows, maintained → dim, peeling paint
- Training area: active dummy + full rack → fallen dummy + empty rack
- Ground: packed dirt path → same dirt but with weeds and cracks near the wall gaps

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: thornbriar wall tiles (dense green hedge, 1 tile high, with flower accents for father's era), dead thornbriar variant (gray-brown, gaps showing background through), wooden gate tiles (vertical planks, iron hinges — open and closed states), guardhouse tiles (stone base, wood upper, small window), dirt path tiles (packed brown earth), training dummy tile (wooden cross-frame with straw), weapons rack tile (wooden frame with sword/shield silhouettes). The thornbriar should look impenetrable — dark green with thorns visible at pixel level. Father's era: thornbriar has tiny white flower pixels scattered on top. Son's era: same structure but desaturated with brown-gray dead patches, some tiles have visible gaps (1-tile holes showing the ground behind).
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: thornbriar rustling in wind (constant, leafy), gate creaking on hinges when opened, militia guard footsteps on dirt, training dummy being struck (wooden thwack), distant market sounds from the south, birdsong from beyond the wall. Son's era: wind through thornbriar gaps (higher pitch, whistling), gate groaning (it never fully closes), a single guard's footsteps, creature sounds from beyond the wall (distant, indistinct — could be animal, could be worse), no market sounds from the south.
creative_prompts.music: Shares the Thornwall theme but at lower volume, transitioning. Father's era: the Market folk melody fading as you move north, replaced by a single sustained note on a low flute — the edge of safety. Son's era: near-silence with a quiet, repeating two-note motif on plucked strings (tension, watchfulness). When facing north toward the gate exit, a faint preview of the Whispering Woods theme bleeds in.

template_properties.overview: The deep interior of the Whispering Woods, where ancient trees block most sunlight and the canopy trail provides the only safe path above the tangled undergrowth. The Moss Clearing — a natural depression where foresters camp — sits at the center. The path north exits toward the Iron Peaks Trail. This zone has denser encounters and more exploration than the Edge. The Ancient Stones (carved markers from a forgotten civilization) are found here, hinting at the buried structures beneath the forest.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Large zone (24x24 tiles). Dense tree coverage — only 40% of the ground is walkable. The canopy trail (elevated wooden walkway tiles) provides a safe route through the treetops in the northern half. The Moss Clearing is a 6x6 open area at the center with soft green moss tiles and a campfire ring. The Deep Root (the oldest tree, 3x3 tiles) is in the northwest corner. Ancient Stones are scattered as 1x1 interactable tiles at 4-5 locations among the roots. Paths are narrow and winding — no straight lines. A stream from the Edge zone continues through the southeast corner. Exit south: back to Edge. Exit north: to Iron Peaks Trail.
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- 2-3 passive creatures (foxes, owls)
- 1-2 hostile creatures (wolves, territorial)
- Forester camp at Moss Clearing (rest point, campfire save)
- Ancient Stone interactions (examine → lore text, Starweaver Lens reveals hidden inscriptions)

**Son's era:**
- 3-4 hostile corrupted creatures (stronger than Edge encounters)
- Sinkhole at Moss Clearing center (where the campfire was)
- Canopy trail sections collapsed (requires alternate ground routes)
- Ancient Stones now glow faintly (the buried structures are activating)
- The Deep Root is stressed — sap weeping, bark cracked
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Moss Clearing: green moss, campfire ring → sinkhole at center, moss blackened at edges
- Canopy trail: intact wooden walkways → 2-3 sections collapsed (fallen plank tiles)
- Ancient Stones: inert, covered in moss → faint violet glow, moss burned away
- Deep Root: massive healthy trunk → cracked bark, sap pools, still alive but visibly damaged
- Tree density: unchanged, but many trees show stress (bark peeling, canopy thinning)
- Stream: clear → cloudy with a faint violet tint near sinkhole
- Ground: thick moss → patches of black fungal growth, crystal shards near sinkhole

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: dense forest floor (dark green moss, minimal light), massive tree trunks (3-4 tile wide ancient trees — these should feel enormous), canopy overlay (very dark green, heavy coverage, only path tiles are visible beneath), canopy trail tiles (wooden planks suspended between trees, lighter than ground level), Moss Clearing tiles (bright green moss, lighter than surrounding forest, clearly a break in the canopy), campfire ring tiles (stone circle with ash/embers), Deep Root tiles (enormous trunk with visible root system spreading 3x3, bark texture detailed at pixel level), Ancient Stone tiles (gray carved stone half-buried in roots, small — 1x1 with inscribed line details). Son's era additions: collapsed walkway tiles (broken planks hanging at angles), sinkhole tiles (dark void with crystal-violet edge glow), glowing Ancient Stone variant (same stone but with violet light pixels emanating), cracked Deep Root (sap drip pixels, bark gap patterns), black fungal moss (replacing green moss near corruption sources), crystal shard ground tiles (small violet geometric shapes on dark ground). The zone should feel claustrophobic — limited visibility, heavy canopy, narrow paths between massive trunks.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: deep forest layered soundscape. The whispering canopy at full volume — a constant, almost conversational murmur. Owl hoots (night cycle), fox barks (distant), wolf howl (rare, far away), thick undergrowth rustling, heavy branch creaking from ancient trees, stream (muffled by distance and canopy), footsteps on moss (soft, damp), footsteps on wooden walkway (hollow clonk). At Moss Clearing: campfire crackle, the canopy rustle is quieter (opening in trees). Son's era: the whisper is louder and higher-pitched — urgent, warning. Crystal resonance hum near the sinkhole and glowing stones (a sustained, slightly pulsing tone). Corrupted creature sounds (guttural, wrong-sounding versions of normal animals). Collapsed walkway: wood groaning when walked near. Deep Root: a slow, deep creak like a ship hull under pressure. Sinkhole: a low draft sound, as if air is being pulled downward.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: the Whispering Woods theme fully developed — dual recorders, lute, with added low drone from a bowed psaltery. Darker and more mysterious than the Edge variant. The melody moves in a minor key with modal inflections suggesting ancient, pre-human music. The forest sounds are heavily blended into the arrangement — the boundary between music and place dissolves. Loop: 150 seconds. Son's era: the recorder is gone. The psaltery drone dominates, with crystal-chime percussion (representing the corruption) replacing the rhythmic lute. A solo low flute plays fragments of the melody — not the full tune, just broken phrases, as if the forest itself is forgetting its song. Near the sinkhole: all melodic content drops out, leaving only the drone and crystal chimes. Loop: 150 seconds.

template_properties.overview: The first zone of the Whispering Woods, where the village farmlands give way to old-growth forest. A dirt path from Thornwall's north gate enters from the south and splits into a main trail heading north (to the Deep Woods) and a side path east (to the Forester's Cabin where Maren lives). The canopy is lighter here than deeper in — enough sunlight reaches the ground for wildflowers and undergrowth. This zone serves as the first wilderness area with light combat encounters.
template_properties.layout_and_terrain: Large zone (24x20 tiles). The southern third is transitional — scattered trees, tall grass, the edge of Thornwall's abandoned farmland (son's era: fallow fields with sinkholes). The middle is proper forest edge — medium-density trees with a clear path winding through. The northern third is denser canopy with the path narrowing. The dirt path splits at center: main path continues north, side path goes east to the Forester's Cabin (a small structure at the map's east edge). A stream crosses the zone east-west near the north end. Walkable tiles: dirt path, grass, shallow stream crossing. Impassable: tree trunks, dense undergrowth, deep stream sections.
template_properties.entities_and_encounters:
**Father's era:**
- 1-2 passive forest creatures (deer, rabbit — ambient)
- 1 forester NPC on the path (dialogue: forest conditions, path advice)
- Medicinal herb pickups near the stream (3-4 spots)

**Son's era:**
- 1-2 hostile corrupted creatures (first combat encounters)
- Maren (near Forester's Cabin — companion recruitment)
- Fewer herb spots (some replaced by fungal growth — visual cue for corruption)
- 1 sinkhole near abandoned farmland (environmental hazard / story point)
template_properties.era_variants:
**Father → Son changes:**
- Southern farmland: golden wheat → cracked fallow earth with 1 sinkhole tile
- Tree canopy: full green → thinned, some bare branches letting harsh light through
- Stream: clear blue → slightly murky gray-blue
- Wildflowers: present → mostly gone, replaced by dark fungal patches
- Forester's Cabin: well-maintained → patched up, Maren's modifications visible
- Path condition: clear dirt → overgrown edges, some roots breaking through
- Creature type: passive animals → hostile corrupted creatures

creative_prompts.tilemap-art: 16-bit pixel art tileset, top-down perspective, 16x16 tile grid. Core tiles: dirt path (brown, worn, 2-3 variants), grass ground (varied greens with occasional wildflower pixel), tree trunk tiles (brown circles of varying size — small 1x1, medium 2x1), canopy overlay tiles (semi-transparent green dappled pattern, layered above ground), undergrowth tiles (dark green, impassable, shorter than trees), stream tiles (2-tile wide, animated blue with white highlight pixels for flow), stream crossing tile (shallow, walkable, slightly different color), tall grass tiles (lighter green, swaying animation frame). Forester's Cabin: small wooden structure (3x2), chimney smoke, pelts hanging outside. Son's era additions: dead tree trunk tiles (gray), thinned canopy overlay (more gaps), fungal growth tiles (dark purple-black patches on ground), sinkhole tile (dark circle with crumbled edge), cracked earth tiles (for abandoned farmland), murky stream variant. The visual transition from south (open, light) to north (dense, darker) should be gradual across the zone.
creative_prompts.ambience: Father's era: the signature Whispering Woods canopy rustle (constant, layered leaf sounds), birdsong (3-4 species at varying distances), stream babbling (louder near water, fading with distance), insect buzz (gentle, summer), footsteps switching from dirt to grass to shallow water. Occasional: deer movement in undergrowth, branch snap, woodpecker. A full, healthy forest soundscape. Son's era: canopy rustle still present but higher-pitched and thinner. Fewer bird species (1-2, with long silences between calls). Stream sound is present but duller. Add: wood creaking (stressed trees), a low subsonic pulse near the sinkhole, crystal-like chiming near fungal patches (faint, unsettling). Creature sounds: skittering in undergrowth, a low growl at medium distance. The forest edge should sound like a warning — enough nature sounds to be recognizably forest, enough wrong sounds to signal danger.
creative_prompts.music: Father's era: layered woodwinds in E minor — two recorders in thirds over finger-picked lute. Gentle, cyclical, walking pace. Natural sounds bleed into the arrangement. The melody is exploratory — ascending phrases suggesting discovery. Loop: 120 seconds. Son's era: solo recorder, same melody but with pauses where the second recorder should be. Lute replaced by bowed psaltery adding tension. Occasional dissonant note — a half-step clash that resolves quickly, suggesting the corruption is audible. Near the sinkhole: music drops to just the psaltery drone. Loop: 120 seconds.

template_properties.overview: A regional military force that patrols the territories between the coastal cities and the Iron Peaks. The Iron Vanguard was established a century ago to protect trade routes from bandits and mountain creatures. In Kaelen's era, they are a professional but overstretched garrison — competent soldiers led by pragmatic officers. In Aric's era, twenty years of escalating threats have transformed them into a hardened, suspicious force that controls movement through the region and views independent adventurers as liabilities rather than assets.
template_properties.purpose_and_goals: Father's era: maintain trade route safety, patrol the Iron Peaks foothills, and respond to threats reported by settlements like Thornwall. They are reactive — they deal with problems as they appear. Kaelen has a complicated relationship with the Vanguard: they respect his results but resent his independence. Son's era: the Vanguard has expanded its mandate. Faced with creatures emerging from the mountains and the collapse of the northern farmlands, they have established checkpoints, restricted travel, and begun forcibly relocating smaller settlements. Their goal has shifted from protection to control. They view the tremors as a military problem requiring military solutions and are deeply skeptical of anyone claiming it requires scholarship (the Academy) or exploration (Aric).
template_properties.hierarchy_and_structure:
- **Commander** — a single regional commander based in a fortress south of the Iron Peaks. Father's era: Commander Reva, a veteran who respects results over protocol. Son's era: Commander Dain, her successor, who favors order over flexibility.
- **Captains** — field officers commanding garrisons of 20-50 soldiers. Each controls a section of road or a checkpoint.
- **Soldiers** — rank-and-file troops, mostly conscripts from coastal cities who serve three-year terms.
- **Scouts** — light infantry who patrol the mountain foothills. The most competent branch, often recruited from local populations.

Total strength: approximately 300 in father's era, 500 in son's era (expanded through conscription).
template_properties.territory_and_influence: The Vanguard controls the main road network between the coast and the mountains. Their fortress sits at the crossroads south of the Iron Peaks. In father's era, their control is light — patrols, toll stations, and a few small garrisons. In son's era, they have established hard checkpoints that require travel permits, fortified the crossroads into a walled compound, and begun patrolling deep into the Whispering Woods (much to Maren's fury). They do not control Thornwall directly, but their nearest checkpoint is close enough that Thornwall's trade depends on Vanguard goodwill.

creative_prompts.emblem: 16-bit pixel art icon (16x16 and 32x32 variants). A stylized iron mountain peak with a horizontal sword crossing it at the midpoint. The mountain is angular and sharp — three pointed peaks. The sword is straight and functional, not ornate. Color palette: iron gray mountain on a dark red field, steel-colored sword. The design communicates strength and rigidity. Used on soldier shields, checkpoint flags, travel permits, and branded onto Vanguard-issued equipment.
creative_prompts.theme-music: Military march in C minor — snare drum and low brass (trombone, tuba). The rhythm is strict and regular, emphasizing discipline and uniformity. A trumpet plays a short, declarative melody — four bars, repeated. No ornamentation, no improvisation. Father's era variant: the march is played at moderate tempo with a workmanlike quality — soldiers on patrol, doing a job. Son's era variant: the same march at a slightly faster tempo with heavier percussion, an added bass drum, and the trumpet melody played by multiple horns in unison — louder, more authoritarian, the sound of an organization that has grown beyond its original purpose.
creative_prompts.color-palette: Primary: iron gray (#6B6E70) — armor, weapons, practical equipment. Secondary: deep crimson (#8B2E2E) — banners, officer cloaks, authority markings. Accent: dark steel (#3D3F42) — shadows, fortification walls. Highlight: forge orange (#C47A30) — torchlight, campfires, the warm color of a working garrison. In son's era, the orange shifts cooler and the crimson deepens, suggesting the Vanguard has become less warm and more imposing. The palette is heavy and grounded — no pastels, no bright colors, everything suggests metal, stone, and fire.

template_properties.overview: The scholarly order that operates the Academy of Starlight. Once a powerful institution that advised kings and predicted harvests, the Starlight Scholars have dwindled to a handful of aging academics and a few young students. They are knowledge-keepers, not warriors — their power lies in information, celestial cartography, and access to the Academy's vast archives. They are politically neutral but practically isolated, clinging to relevance in a world that has largely moved on from star-reading.
template_properties.purpose_and_goals: The Scholars' stated mission is to preserve and expand the celestial knowledge left by the Starweaver founders. In practice, they are divided: Aldric's faction prioritizes safety and controlled access to knowledge, while a smaller group of younger scholars (including Sera) believes the Academy must actively use its knowledge to address the growing crisis — the tremors, the dying forests, the sealed caverns. The Scholars do not seek political power, but their archives contain information that every other faction wants.
template_properties.hierarchy_and_structure:
- **Elder** — Aldric holds this title reluctantly. The Elder has sole access to the Archive Vaults and final say on what knowledge is shared externally.
- **Starweavers** — senior scholars who can use the Starweaver Lenses. Only Aldric truly qualifies; two others claim the title based on seniority.
- **Archivists** — scholars who maintain and catalogue the library. They handle external requests for information.
- **Students** — apprentice scholars studying under Starweavers. Sera is the most advanced.

Total membership: approximately fifteen in father's era, eight in son's era.
template_properties.territory_and_influence: The Scholars control the Academy campus and the cliff it sits on. They have no military presence and rely on their remote location and reputation for protection. Their influence is informational: traders, military leaders, and other factions occasionally send envoys to request specific knowledge (crop predictions, navigation charts, historical records). The Scholars trade information for supplies and funding. In the son's era, these requests have dried up as the Academy's reputation has faded.

creative_prompts.emblem: 16-bit pixel art icon (16x16 and 32x32 variants). A stylized eye with a six-pointed star as the pupil, set within a circle of tiny dots representing constellations. The eye is open and upward-facing, suggesting observation of the sky. Color palette: pale blue star on white eye, silver-gray constellation dots, dark navy background circle. The design is geometric and precise — scholarly, not mystical. Used on Academy banners, scholar robes (embroidered on the collar), and official documents.
creative_prompts.theme-music: A chamber ensemble piece in E-flat major: harpsichord, string quartet, and glass harmonica. Formal and structured, with a waltz-like rhythm that suggests tradition and ceremony. The harpsichord carries the melody — intricate, slightly ornate, the sound of accumulated knowledge. The glass harmonica enters only in transitions, providing the signature Academy shimmer. The piece feels old — deliberately archaic in its arrangement, like music preserved in amber.
creative_prompts.color-palette: Primary: pale limestone white (#E8E4D8) — the Academy's stone. Secondary: celestial blue (#6B8CAE) — starlight, the lens crystal. Accent: brass gold (#B8963E) — instruments, lens frames, embroidery. Shadow: deep navy (#1A2440) — night sky, Archive Vault darkness. Highlight: silver-white (#D4DCE8) — active starlight, lens glow. The palette is cool and restrained — no warm reds or oranges. Everything suggests moonlight, old stone, and polished metal.

template_properties.overview: A main-arc-tie-in quest in Kaelen's era, set in Saltmere Port. Three weeks into Kaelen's journey toward the Iron Peaks, he is detoured to Saltmere by a message from Elder Aldric: fishing boats have been going down in the shallows near the chalk river estuary, and the survivors are describing their hulls breaking on something invisible. Aldric believes — based on his fragmentary star-charts and a pattern he has seen only once before — that whatever is shaking the Iron Peaks is also shifting the rock beneath the coastal seabed, and that the two phenomena are the same crisis at different depths. Kaelen arrives in Saltmere to find Harbormaster Cael Vire running out of patience and options, and the port's working class running out of income. His help is requested. He does not have time to refuse.
template_properties.narrative_arc:
**Act 1 — The Harbor That Is Losing Boats.** Kaelen arrives at Saltmere Port in the late afternoon and takes a room at the Saltscale Inn. Brine serves him supper and, without being asked, tells him what everyone in the port already knows: three fishing boats in the last month, all in the same stretch of shallows near the chalk estuary, all breaking up without warning in calm weather. Two crews survived by swimming; one did not. Cael Vire is at the back table. She invites Kaelen over after he has finished his stew. She has heard from Aldric. She is not happy about it. She will take the help.
